subject: ACCN
title: Intensive Care Nursing: Foundations of Intensive Care Nursing
description: This introductory course will explore foundational aspects of intensive care nursing practice. Intensive care practice, ethical and legal issues, and psychosocial, educational and cultural aspects of intensive care nursing will be discussed to gain context of the intensive care nursing role. The principles of physiology pertinent to intensive care nursing will be reviewed to lay the foundation for a more in-depth exploration of disease pathology and interventions. These include a review of cellular, pulmonary, cardiovascular, neurological, gastrointestinal, genitourinary and defence physiology.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ACCN
title: Intensive Care Nursing: Pathophysiology
description: Principles of physiology, first addressed in Advanced Studies in Critical Care Nursing 5501 are applied to critical illness. Emphasis is placed on breathing, circulation, neurologic renal and body defence of the critically ill within the intensive care unit to illustrate organ dysfunction and illness experiences. Therapeutics for each of the five systems are discussed including mechanical ventilation and pharmacotherapy.

subject: ACCN
title: Intensive Care Nursing: Pharmacology for the Acutely III
description: Principles and physiological mechanisms related to pharmacology are addressed. Emphasis is placed on a thorough understanding of major drug categories and current medications frequently used by the intensive care nurse for acute and critically ill patients. Pharmacological considerations are addressed in relation to the management and prevention of complications.

subject: ACCN
title: Intensive Care Nursing and Emergency Nursing: Assessment
description: Examines various aspects of assessment within a physiologic systems framework, particular to acute/critically ill patients. Emphasizes analysis and integration of physical, laboratory and computer derived findings.
notes: Transfer credit for this course may be given to students who have national certification in either Intensive Care Nursing or Emergency Nursing. Transfer credit for this course may be given to students who have national certification in either Intensive Care Nursing or Emergency Nursing.

subject: ACCN
title: Emergency Nursing - Pathophysiology
description: Provides a thorough review of physiological and pathophysiological mechanisms and their relationships to human body systems across the age span. Facilitates study of mechanisms underlying selected clinical conditions that are common and/or emergent in the emergency setting. Uses pathophysiology as a basis for understanding clinical conditions in order to guide relevant history taking, assessment and management of patients with these conditions.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ACCN
title: Emergency Nursing: Care of the Acutely III and Injured - Part A
description: This is the first of a two-part course, which describes the nursing care of the acutely ill and injured patients in the emergency department. There is an emphasis on pathophysiology as a basis for assessing, triaging, planning, implementing and evaluating care (the nursing process). The course begins with a discussion of factors common to all patients and/or emergency departments. The remaining units focus on acute illnesses and injuries for selected body systems for adults. Special considerations for elderly and children are also presented. Illness and injuries are grouped according to the patient’s presenting complaint or predominant physiologic abnormality.

subject: ACCN
title: Emergency Nursing: Care of the Acutely III and Injured - Part B
description: This is the second of a two-part course series on emergency nursing. As with part A, the nursing process and pathophysiology are the basis for the management of adults and children with illnesses and injuries affecting the body systems not previously discussed. Additional topics include management of other emergencies along with evidence based practice and multisystem/multitrauma disorders.

subject: ACCN
title: Intensive Care Nursing: Clinical
description: Other Hour(s): 16 Other Hours Schedule Type: Clinical The clinical practicum provides the student with opportunities to integrate and apply intensive care nursing skills and theory in clinical practice. Students gain hands-on experience with a variety of assessment and clinical interventions. Nurses with intensive care nursing experience may apply for prior learning credit.
notes: Evidence of 1-year full-time equivalent (FTE) acute care medical/surgical experience (within the last 3-5 years) is required. If you do not meet the experiential requirement, a letter to support how you meet this requirement may be considered.

subject: ACCN
title: Emergency Nursing: Clinical
description: Other Hour(s): 16 Other Hours Schedule Type: Clinical This clinical practicum provides the student with opportunities to integrate and apply emergency nursing theory and skills in the clinical setting. Students gain hands-on experience with a variety of assessment and clinical interventions. Nurses with emergency experience may apply for prior learning credit.
notes: Evidence of 1-year full-time equivalent (FTE) acute care medical/surgical experience (within the last 3-5 years) is required. If you do not meet the experiential requirement, a letter to support how you meet this requirement may be considered.

subject: ACCT
title: Financial Accounting Concepts
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course provides an introduction to accounting principles and the accounting cycle. Topics include fundamental concepts such as accounting principles, assumptions, constraints, and the preparation and reporting of financial information to investors, managers, and others. Emphasis is on financial statement preparation and analysis.
notes: Only one of ACCT 2120 and ACCT 2121 may be used for graduation purposes from the BBA Only one of ACCT 2120 and ACCT 2121 may be used for graduation purposes from the BBA

subject: ACCT
title: Directed Readings
description: Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Directed Reading Subject to the approval of the Chair. Directed Readings are intended to provide a more flexible approach for students who want to pursue and receive credit in areas of study which are of particular interest to them. Two Directed Reading courses can be used for graduation purposes but they must be in different disciplines. A Directed Reading cannot replicate an existing course. The objectives of the Directed Reading course(s) must be filed in the Office of the Registrar and will be made available to any institution requesting them for evaluation purposes. Arrangements for Directed Readings must be completed on or before the Add/ Drop deadline.

subject: ACCT
title: Financial Accounting Practices
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course builds on the concepts and skills learned in introductory accounting and prepares the student for the more conceptual approach required for Intermediate Accounting. Students learn to apply the accounting framework, and further develop skills for recording transactions and events, and analyzing financial statements. Topics include revenue recognition, long-term liabilities, partnership accounting, shareholders’ equity, investments, and the cash flow statement.

subject: ACCT
title: Intermediate Accounting I
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course examines issues and problems related to GAAP for income determination and revenue recognition. International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS) and Accounting standards for Private Enterprises (ASPE) relating to recognition, measurement, and financial statement presentation of assets are addressed.

subject: ACCT
title: Management Accounting I
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course provides an introduction to the nature, scope and use of managerial accounting. Topics include management planning and control, inventory and cost flows, standard costs and variance analysis, budgeting, cost-volume-profit analysis, relevant costs and decision making.

subject: ACCT
title: Oil and Gas Accounting
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Students are provided with an introduction to the accounting principles and practices associated with oil and gas exploration and production. The impact of income and other taxes on the industry is also considered.

subject: ACCT
title: Management Accounting II
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course examines various costing methods and analytical tools used by management accountants to provide information and recommendations relevant to short-term and long-term decision making. Topics covered include capital budgeting, cost and revenue allocation, profitability variances, process costing, and inventory management.

subject: ACCT
title: Business Sustainability Reporting
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course provides students with an introduction to, and a critical analysis of, current practices in business Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) Sustainability reporting in Canada and worldwide. Topics covered include corporate sustainability reporting practices, sustainability regulatory requirements, reporting standards and frameworks, data sources and management, rating agencies, and investment products. The course also introduces the use of corporate sustainability disclosures in financial and valuation analyses. The class format combines lectures, interactive discussions, case studies, group projects, analysis and discussions, and project presentations.

subject: ACCT
title: Accounting Information Systems
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 This course provides a comprehensive overview of accounting information systems (AIS) and their role in modern accounting practices. Students will explore the interplay between accounting processes and information technology, with a focus on risk management, internal controls, software systems, and data analysis. Key topics include risk assessment, system design, and emerging technologies, with practical hands-on experience using QuickBooks software. By the end of the course, students will be proficient in managing accounting systems and leveraging data for strategic decision-making.

subject: ACCT
title: Special Topics in Accounting
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course provides an examination of selected topics in Accounting. As this is a variable content course, the specific topic will be announced and advertised each time it is offered.
notes: Credit will be allowed for only one offering of ACCT 3730. Students will not receive credit for this course more than once.

subject: ACCT
title: Directed Readings
description: Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Directed Reading Subject to the approval of the Chair. Directed Readings are intended to provide a more flexible approach for students who want to pursue and receive credit in areas of study which are of particular interest to them. Two Directed Reading courses can be used for graduation purposes but they must be in different disciplines. A Directed Reading cannot replicate an existing course. The objectives of the Directed Reading course(s) must be filed in the Office of the Registrar and will be made available to any institution requesting them for evaluation purposes. Arrangements for Directed Readings must be completed on or before the Add/ Drop deadline.

subject: ACCT
title: Intermediate Accounting II
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course provides an in-depth analysis of accounting theory and principles related to the reporting of short-term and long-term liabilities, leases, pensions, corporate income taxes, shareholders’ equity, complex financial instruments, earnings per share, the cash flow statement, and accounting changes and error analysis. International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS) and Accounting Standards for Private Enterprises (ASPE) relating to recognition, measurement, and financial statement presentation of liabilities and equity are addressed.

subject: ACCT
title: Principles of Auditing
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course examines the role of the internal or external auditor in business and explores the legal and ethical environment of auditing. Emphasis is placed on methods and procedures used in auditing and reporting as they relate to specific financial statement areas such as assets, liabilities, equities, revenues, and expenses. Other topics covered include internal control, risk and materiality, and the auditor’s responsibility with respect to fraud.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ACCT
title: Introduction to Personal and Corporate Income Tax
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course covers Canadian income tax laws affecting individuals and corporations. Topics include the computation of individual and corporate income tax, taxable income, taxes payable, corporate distributions, taxation of capital gains,and the preparation of corporate and personal tax returns.

subject: ACCT
title: Advanced Financial Accounting
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This is a senior level course in financial accounting, which covers the following areas: intercompany investments, joint ventures, accounting for not-for-profit organizations, and translation of foreign currency transactions and financial statements.

subject: ACCT
title: Advanced Managerial Accounting
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course examines advanced management accounting practices designed to help managers formulate and implement new strategies, motivate and evaluate organizational performance, and achieve operational excellence. This course emphasizes current management accounting practices within a variety of business environments using a case approach. These practices include the theory of constraints, activity-based management, cost-based decision making, and the balanced scorecard.

subject: ACCT
title: Accounting Theory
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course provides an overview of contemporary accounting theory and research, and explores how these theories and research are applied in the development and practice of the accounting profession. Emerging, thought-provoking issues in the field of accounting are discussed.

subject: ACCT
title: Advanced Topics in Accounting
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This capstone course in Accounting integrates concepts learned in earlier core accounting courses with accounting theory, corporate governance, strategy, sustainability, corporate social responsibility and business ethics. A case-analysis approach is used to identify issues relating to financial and managerial accounting, audit and taxation, evaluate alternatives and exercise professional judgment in making recommendations. Students will apply advanced financial statement analysis to the corporate annual report and critically examine the application of the accounting rules and policies by the company.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: AIRF
title: Introduction to Aviation Navigation
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course introduces students to aviation navigation in preparation for their Commercial Pilot’s License. It is designed in part to provide students with the knowledge to not only achieve the Transport Canada requirements respecting navigation and aviation operations but to develop a broader understanding of air navigation.

subject: AIRF
title: Introduction to Aviation Meteorology (Introductory)
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course meets the meteorology requirements for the Department of Transport Commercial Pilot’s Licence, basic meteorology as it applies to aviation and the ability to interpret weather information for pilots as supplied by Atmospheric Environment Services.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: AIRF
title: Aircraft Engines and Systems
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course is designed to give students a theoretical, practical working knowledge for aircraft piston and turbine engines and ancillary controls, as well as Aircraft Systems.

subject: AIRF
title: Aviation History and Air Law
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course is a wide ranging examination of the Aviation Regulations, including the structure of the regulatory system, the organization of the Canadian Aviation Regulations and the Rules of the Air. Students will also explore the historical context of the regulatory and legal environment within the Aviation Industry, including an examination of contractual issues specific to aviation business. The Course explores the legal issues that confront pilots and the Aviation Industry both in Canada and Internationally.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: AIRF
title: Aeronautics, Industry and Flight Operations
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course develops the students’ understanding of aeronautics, the aviation Industry and Flight Operations within an Operational Flight Organization. Topics covered include TC required subjects covering Physics of Flight, and Flight Operations. The course will further cover Aviation Business Fundamentals, Financial Models, Corporate Strategy and Competition, Professional and Ethical issues and the present and future state of the Aviation Industry in Canada and Internationally.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: AIRF
title: Advanced Aviation Meteorology
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course prepares students for the meteorological portion of the Instrument Flying Test. It deals with practical flight planning problems, both low level and high level. Upon successful completion of the course students should have a good grounding in practical meteorological problems confronting an aircraft captain.

subject: AIRF
title: Human Factors, Safety and Aviation Technology
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course is designed to give students a thorough understanding of the Human Factors involved in pilot decision making as well as future aircraft technologies that assist pilots in maintaining safe flight operations. SMS Systems, Maintenance safety Systems and Air Operations organizational structures will be studied for their combined role in flight operations safety. Case studies and aeromedical data will be used to support the students understanding of the physiological effects of unusual attitudes and meteorological phenomena on a pilots performance. New and future Aircraft technologies will be discussed, including avionics, engines and airframes that advance pilot performance and safety.

subject: AIRF
title: Directed Readings
description: Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Directed Reading Subject to the approval of the Chair. Directed Readings are intended to provide a more flexible approach for students who want to pursue and receive credit in areas of study which are of particular interest to them. Two Directed Reading courses can be used for graduation purposes but they must be in different disciplines. A Directed Reading cannot replicate an existing course. The objectives of the Directed Reading course(s) must be filed in the Office of the Registrar and will be made available to any institution requesting them for evaluation purposes. Arrangements for Directed Readings must be completed on or before the Add/ Drop deadline.

subject: AIRF
title: Multi-Engine Instrument Rating
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course is designed to introduce students to the theory and practise of advanced instrument flying. The principles of IFR flying will be discussed, as well as an in depth review of the Canadian Aviation Regulations pertaining to IFR Instrument Flying. The material in Transport Canada’s Study and Reference Guide for the Instrument Rating (INRAT) Will be covered, preparing the student for their TC Instrument written test (INRAT) and their TC Multi Engine-IFR Flight Test.

subject: AIRF
title: CRM-TEM and Two Crew Coordination
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course is designed to prepare the student for industry by learning to apply practical Crew Resource Management and Threat and Error Management skills, (CRM-TEM) in maintaining safe flight operations. The students will further be put through two crew simulator sessions where they will become proficient in applying CRM-TEM concepts in the flightdeck while enhancing their operational effectiveness in two crew flight operations. The Course strongly emphasizes Situational Awareness, and Active Communication skills, and effective systems monitoring in the modern flightdeck.

subject: AIRF
title: Advanced Aviation Psychology and Preparing for Industry
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course gives students an in-depth understanding of the psychological factors involved in two crew and single pilot decision making and how the human brain functions under stress. Three Point Operational Discipline (TPODS) and Higher Order Thinking Skills (HOTS) will be taught to give the pilot effective situational awareness skills to mitigate incidents and increase safe flight operations. Aircraft Accident Case Studies will be used to identify the processes and decisions the aircrew and the organization made leading to the incident, with a focus on mitigation. Students will be trained in effective interview skills, including mock industry interview panels, to prepare them for the industry.

subject: AIRF
title: Directed Readings
description: Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Directed Reading Subject to the approval of the Chair. Directed Readings are intended to provide a more flexible approach for students who want to pursue and receive credit in areas of study which are of particular interest to them. Two Directed Reading courses can be used for graduation purposes but they must be in different disciplines. A Directed Reading cannot replicate an existing course. The objectives of the Directed Reading course(s) must be filed in the Office of the Registrar and will be made available to any institution requesting them for evaluation purposes. Arrangements for Directed Readings must be completed on or before the Add/ Drop deadline.

subject: AIRF
title: Airline Operations and Management
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course provides the student with a solid foundation in multiple and complex facets involved in airline management. Subjects will focus on the increasing use of data analytics in determining airline strategic decisions involved in route demand analysis, scheduling optimization, competitive slot times, fleet planning, aircraft leasing, maintenance scheduling and fleet modernization decision-making. The course will further develop the student’s skills in analyzing and creating profit plans in both passenger and cargo departments. Human resources, crew scheduling, executive management, shareholder and board reporting, and regulatory compliance and safety issues within management will be further discussed. Students will be introduced to the increasing pressure on airlines to limit carbon emissions through technological advancements and route structure design. A strong focus will be placed on the student’s understanding of managing safety management and fatigue risk management systems as well as thoroughly understanding flight dispatch operations. Practical time spent in an Operational Dispatch Department and in tracking SMS/FRMS data to implement operational management changes will be undertaken.

subject: AIRF
title: Airport Management
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course will prepare students to undertake airport operations and management roles. Students will learn how airports are planned and developed and how revenue is planned and created through an in-depth understanding of each role and department within airport operations. Students will further understand labour relations, air transport industry negotiations, airport marketing, cargo and logistics hubs, and safety operations, as well as environmental, social, and governance issues related to airport management. AVSEC (Aviation Security Systems and Measures) and SMS systems will be reviewed.

subject: AIRF
title: Air Cargo Supply Chain Logistics
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course will give the student a more in-depth understanding of the economic impact of airlines and airports on the global economy, airline pricing and revenues, airline and airport corporate strategic financial planning, the international regulatory framework, the impact of deregulation, the geo-politics of aviation, airport economics and its integration with airlines, the environmental issues, global airport financial models, and challenges facing the air transport industry. The course will further review the roles of IATA, TIACA, ICAO, and various labour unions and management relations that affect air transport economics. The impact of key global disruptions such as Covid-19, airspace closures, and other geo-political events on air transport economic models will be reviewed in detail to build data-informed models and to seek risk mitigation strategies to limit financial damage to the airline and airport sectors and to the regional and global economies.

subject: AIRF
title: Air Transport Economics/Global Aviation Business
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course will give students a more in-depth understanding of the economic impact of airlines on the global economy, airline pricing and revenues, airline corporate strategic financial planning, the international regulatory framework, the impact of deregulation, the geopolitics of aviation, airport economics and its integration with airlines, the environmental issues, global airport financial models, and challenges facing the air transport industry. The course will further review the roles of IATA, TIACA, ICAO, and various labour unions and management relations that affect airline economics. The impact of key geo-political events such as Covid-19 on airline economic models and future geo-political challenges will be reviewed in detail to build robust models and to seek risk mitigation strategies to limit financial damage to the airline and to the regional and global economies.

subject: AIRF
title: Life Cycle Analysis - Aviation Systems and Programs
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course will introduce students to aircraft and component life cycle analysis and its impact on effective airline operations and revenues. Differences in life cycle analysis and aviation systems and management programs between regional and national international carriers will be reviewed. Students will understand the market in engine leasing and component life cycle acquisition and contracts. Students will have a strong understanding of the global MRO industry and aircraft maintenance cycles and the importance of timing in parts turnover, overhaul, and planning removal from service. Real-world visits to review and analyze the maintenance systems of actual operating airlines will form part of the program.

subject: AIRF
title: Aviation Environmental Social Governance and Corporate Ethics
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course will give students a sound foundation in the environmental, social, and governance issues affecting the global aviation industry. The aviation industry’s attempts to lower carbon emissions through technological advancements, alternative fuels, optimized and integrated next-generation air traffic control, and automation will be reviewed. Global carbon credit mechanisms will be reviewed, as well as various marketing initiatives and mechanisms the industry is employing. Challenges to the aviation industry will also be reviewed, including pressure to reduce mass tourism, and future aviation business models will be analyzed.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: AIRF
title: Aviation Strategic Management
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course prepares students to execute strategic management decisions within the airline environment. Case studies will be used to review issues faced by management in the aviation industry in making strategic decisions to guide the airline to growth and profitability. Timing for fleet expansion, new route structures, joint agreements and alliances will be reviewed. STAR, SkyTeam and OneWorld will be researched.

subject: ANTH
title: Introduction to Primatology and Human Evolution
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course introduces biological anthropology with an emphasis on evolutionary theory as it applies to humans. Primate anatomy and behavioural ecology, paleoanthropology, human biocultural evolution, and modern human diversity will be considered. GNED Cluster 1 - Tier 2

subject: ANTH
title: Introduction to Cultural Anthropology
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course introduces cultural anthropology as a comparative study of cultures, societies, and the diverse ways of being in the world. With a commitment to decolonization, this course seeks to provide knowledge about how social relations, politico-economic systems, and historical forces shape people’s lives and behaviours. GNED Cluster 3 - Tier 2
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ANTH
title: Ancient Peoples and Places
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course introduces anthropological archaeology and the deep history of humankind. Starting with the emergence of key technologies like stone and ceramic artifacts, this course reviews a wide breadth of ancient peoples who practiced hunting and foraging, domesticated plants and animals, and developed distinct forms of architecture, monumentality, towns, and polities around the ancient world. GNED Cluster 3 - Tier 2
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ANTH
title: Introduction to Linguistic Anthropology
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course introduces the broad range of topics relevant to the field of linguistic anthropology including language structure (phonology, morphology, and syntax), the origin and evolution of language, semantics and pragmatics, interrelationships of culture and meaning, and the intersection of gender, race, ethnicity, class, and nationality with language and language use. GNED Cluster 3 - Tier 2
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ANTH
title: Methods in Biological Anthropology
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Lab Hour(s): 3 In this course, students will learn methods and applications in biological anthropology. Topics include population genetics, human variation and adaptation, primate and human anatomy, and paleoanthropology. In the laboratory portion of this course, students will engage in applied exercises.
notes: Only one of ANTH 1102 and ANTH 2102 can be used to satisfy graduation requirements. GNED Cluster 1 - Tier 2 Only one of ANTH 1102 and ANTH 2102 can be used to satisfy graduation requirements. GNED Cluster 1 - Tier 2

subject: ANTH
title: Methods in Archaeology
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Lab Hour(s): 3 This course introduces archaeological field and laboratory methods. Students will engage with concepts such as the archaeological record, context, stratigraphy, survey, GIS, and dating, and archaeological material analyses that include stone and ceramic artifacts, and animal and plant remains. The course also explores professionalism, career paths in archaeology, cultural resource management, and ethical research practices.
notes: Only one of ANTH 1104 and ANTH 2104 can be used to satisfy graduation requirements. GNED Cluster 1 - Tier 2 Only one of ANTH 1104 and ANTH 2104 can be used to satisfy graduation requirements. GNED Cluster 1 - Tier 2
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ANTH
title: Biological Anthropology of South Asia
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course offers an overview of prehistoric human dispersals, trends in human evolution, and biological variation among South Asian populations. Emphasis will be placed upon the fossil record, archaeological evidence, and recent morphological, genetic and linguistic diversity of South Asians.

subject: ANTH
title: Field Studies in Anthropology
description: Other Hours Schedule Type: Field Work 3 week field school This course provides an opportunity to examine selected topics in anthropology through field studies, where students will engage in the application of anthropological concepts, methods and techniques. As a variable content course, the specific topic and location will be announced each time this course is offered.
notes: Students will be responsible for paying all costs, including travel associated with the field school experience. GNED Cluster 3 - Tier 3

subject: ANTH
title: Archaeology of North America
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course is a general survey of the prehistory of the North American continent north of Mexico, beginning with the earliest evidence for human occupation in the western hemisphere and ending with the period of European contact. Selected geographical and cultural areas include the Arctic, the Northwest Coast, California, the Southwest, the Eastern Woodlands, and the Prairie-Plains. GNED Cluster 3 - Tier 3
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ANTH
title: Cross-Cultural Communication and Business
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course explores ways in which differences in cultural patterns of communication affect interaction between business parties, particularly those of differing cultural/national/ethnic backgrounds.

subject: ANTH
title: Indigenous People and the State in the Canadian Context
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course considers the history of state relations with Indigenous people in Canada, First Nations, Inuit, and Métis. Topics include the development of “Indian policy” in Canada and sociocultural and legal aspects of current issues such as land claims, treaties, the status of women, human rights, the environment, data sovereignty, and expression of Indigenous identity.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ANTH
title: Anthropological Encounters on the Northwest Coast
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course is a selective survey of the ethnographic representations of the Indigenous peoples of the Northwest Coast that have shaped anthropological knowledge, theory, and method. Topics may include anthropological and Indigenous perspectives on Indigenous languages, subsistence, sociopolitical organization, religious and artistic expression, gendered identities, colonialism and post-colonialism, land claims, and the environment. Recommended Preparation: At least one 1000-level course in Anthropology, Archaeology, Psychology or Sociology.

subject: ANTH
title: Hot Topics in Human Evolution
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course is designed to help students think critically about contemporary topics on human biological evolution. Topics may include new fossil discoveries, reinterpreting hominin taxonomy, molecular anthropology, evolutionary medicine, and how technological innovations can help address questions on human evolution and diversity.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ANTH
title: Ancient Civilizations
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course critically examines the theories and evidence behind what is meant by “civilization”, beginning with the origins of agriculture and its impact on social complexity. Several ancient civilizations from around the world will be reviewed.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ANTH
title: The Anthropology of Gender
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course is a comparative analysis of gender as a central feature of social identity and social relations of power. Biological, archaeological, feminist and post-modern approaches to the understanding of women’s and men’s experiences in their historical and cultural varieties form the basis of the course. Case studies will be drawn from western and non-western cultures around the world.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ANTH
title: Ethnography in Anthropology
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Ethnography (“writing culture”) is central to the discipline of Anthropology. Through the reading of complete ethnographic works and critical treatises on ethnography, students will explore the entirety of the ethnographic enterprise: how ethnography allows unique insights into specific cultures, how ethnographic research is designed and carried out, how ethnographic texts are constructed, and how ethnographic representations have been responded to and interpreted.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ANTH
title: Anthropology Through Film
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course takes a historical and theoretical perspective of ethnographic film, the visual medium through which anthropologists and filmmakers have “captured” and presented human cultural experience for almost 100 years. Students will engage in a critical analysis of the representations presented in ethnographic film and consider relationships between the development of the ethnographic film genre and the development of Anthropology as a whole.

subject: ANTH
title: Gifts and Commodities
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course will examine the political and culturally embedded nature of economies by undertaking a comparative analysis of production, distribution and consumption in small-scale and complex societies. There will be a focus on the effects of global capitalism upon local economic systems.

subject: ANTH
title: An Ethnographic Survey of Native North America
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course provides an overview of native cultures of North America, examining relationships among subsistence and settlement patterns, material culture, social and political organization, ideological structures, ceremonial life, mythology and art. Because of the number and diversity of native peoples in North America, “typical” groups from each culture area will form the basis of description and analysis. Recommended Preparation: ANTH 1103
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ANTH
title: Anthropology in the Public Eye
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This discussion-based course focuses on the public’s perception of Anthropology, not content of Anthropology itself. Students will take an anthropological perspective by critically examining and evaluating the relevance, and substance of current topics such as the role of the media, popular perspectives, public engagement, repatriation of mortuary remains, anthropological representation in museums, ethics, and the antiquities trade. GNED Cluster 3 - Tier 2
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ANTH
title: Field Course in Archaeological Techniques I
description: Other Hours Schedule Type: Field Work This course provides an introduction to the practical application of modern field techniques in Archaeology, including excavation and recording and analysis of sites, artifacts, and related materials. The course will require travel to an archaeological site, usually during the summer for three weeks.
notes: Students will be responsible for paying all costs, including travel, associated with the field school experience.

subject: ANTH
title: Field Course in Archaeological Techniques II
description: Other Hours Schedule Type: Field Work This course provides a comprehensive introduction to the practical application of modern field techniques in archaeology, including excavation, recording, and analysis of sites, artifacts, and related material. This course will require travel to an archaeological site, usually during the summer for six weeks.
notes: Students will be responsible for paying all costs, including travel, associated with the field school experience.

subject: ANTH
title: Practicing Anthropology: Skills for Career and Academic Success - Work-Integrated Learning
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Other Hour(s): 1 Other Hours Schedule Type: Experiential Learning This course introduces key skills for career and academic readiness by emphasizing the practical application of anthropological theoretical and research concepts to real-world issues. Each student will be paired with a community partner to complete a micro-internship. This course bridges academic learning with hands-on application, providing students with insights into how anthropological knowledge can assist with their career objectives and educational goals.

subject: ANTH
title: Directed Readings
description: Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Directed Reading Subject to the approval of the Chair. Directed Readings are intended to provide a more flexible approach for students who want to pursue and receive credit in areas of study which are of particular interest to them. Two Directed Reading courses can be used for graduation purposes but they must be in different disciplines. A Directed Reading cannot replicate an existing course. The objectives of the Directed Reading course(s) must be filed in the Office of the Registrar and will be made available to any institution requesting them for evaluation purposes. Arrangements for Directed Readings must be completed on or before the Add/ Drop deadline.

subject: ANTH
title: From Factory to Gig: A World of Workers
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Anthropology examines how people make a living and how they organize their lives around activities that generate income. Building on the anthropology of work and finance, this course explores the emergence of the worker in the capitalist system and traces various social relations that shape workers’ lives and work alternatives.

subject: ANTH
title: Anthropological Imaginings of Latin America
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course is a selective survey of the ethnographic imaginings and representations of Latin America that have shaped anthropological knowledge, theory and methods. Topics may include post-colonialism, ethnography of violence, gendered identities and sexualities, religious expression, popular culture, and globalization.
notes: There are no enrollment restrictions. ANTH 3205 will be available to all students across campus. GNED Cluster 3 - Tier 3
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ANTH
title: Maya Civilization
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course explores different eras of Indigenous Maya civilization, from its inception 3,000 years ago to its disruption by the Spanish invasion in the 16th century. Through the lens of archaeology and anthropology, students will learn about many aspects of Maya Civilization, such as architecture, trade routes, religion, language, governments, and key figures of ancient Maya history.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ANTH
title: The Anthropology of Religion
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course surveys anthropological approaches to the study of religion. Topics will include religious specialists, such as shamans, sorcerers and priests; practices such as witchcraft, sacrifice, spirit possession, and healing; the role of religion and religious rituals in various cultures; and the ways in which religious belief and practice responds to and promotes social and cultural change.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ANTH
title: Ethnographic Methods
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This class will explore the ways in which ethnographic research is designed, carried out, and analyzed. A variety of ethnographic methods will be discussed, with an emphasis on conducting participant-observation and on in-depth interviewing. Under the guidance of the instructor, students will design, conduct, and report on an ethnographic project of their own choosing.

subject: ANTH
title: Anthropological Perspectives on Globalization
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Anthropologists study globalization to learn how the complex flows of people, goods, capital, and ideas that traverse the globe are understood, experienced, and negotiated by peoples in specific contexts. This class will explore the ways that living in a globalized world affects the lives, sensibilities, and identities of the inhabitants of the so-called “global village “.

subject: ANTH
title: History of Anthropology
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course follows the historical development of Anthropology from early predecessors to its inception as an academic discipline in the nineteenth century and finally to present-day configurations. Emphasis will be placed on the creation of American and Canadian anthropology, the development of specialized approaches within the four fields of anthropology, and the impacts of feminism and postmodernism.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ANTH
title: Statistics for Anthropology
description: Lecture Hour(s): 1 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Tutorial Hour(s): 2 This course offers an introduction to descriptive and inferential statistical techniques as applied to anthropological research. Through instruction and laboratory practice, students will develop a working knowledge of how statistical concepts can be used to correctly analyze, interpret, and evaluate research findings.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ANTH
title: Lithic and Ceramic Analysis
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Lab Hour(s): 3 This course develops students’ practical knowledge of lithic and ceramic technologies-the two most prevalent materials found in archaeological context. Students will spend half of the course in lectures, learning about the key concepts associated with the analysis of both technologies. The other half will be spent in the laboratory, where students will get hands-on, analytical experience with stone and ceramic artifacts, applying the concepts learned in lectures. This course prepares students for field and laboratory archaeological work.

subject: ANTH
title: Ethnography of the Great Plains
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course is a comparative study of peoples of the Great Plains past and present. Focus is upon the material culture, settlement and subsistence patterns, language, social and political structures, gender roles, religion, world view, ritual and art of the Indigenous peoples of the region. Anthropological and ethnohistorical sources will form the primary data, with a comparison to Indigenous sources.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ANTH
title: Human Osteology
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Lab Hour(s): 3 In this intensive lab-based course, students will identify and interpret human skeletal and dental remains using techniques in Biological Anthropology. Assessment of qualitative features, quantitative measurement analyses, and forensic approaches are central components of this field of study. Areas of focus will be functional anatomy, palaeopathology and applied osteology.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ANTH
title: Human Adaptation
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course examines the processes involved in interpreting modern human diversity. Emphasis is placed on biological adaptive responses to particular environments and the cultural influences affecting human diversity. Topics include heat and cold stress, high and low altitude adaptations, skin colour, and physical structure of the human body.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ANTH
title: Zooarchaeology
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Lab Hour(s): 3 In this lab-based course, students will develop a working knowledge of the method, theory, and practice of zooarchaeology. Learning will focus on the skeletal and taxonomic identification of animal remains, quantitative methods, and taphonomy. Students will also explore how zooarchaeological data are used to reconstruct past diet, environment, and society.

subject: ANTH
title: GIS for Anthropology
description: This course introduces students to the potential of GIS for anthropology. Students will first learn how to operate the basic functions of a leading GIS software. Students will also learn how to create databases that are mapped on all sorts of landscapes.
notes: This course will be delivered in a computer lab where a mix of lecture and hands-on computer work will occur.

subject: ANTH
title: Medical Anthropology
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Medical Anthropology explores beliefs, practices and experiences of health, illness and healing in cross-cultural perspective. Topics will include the role of healers, their patients, and the relationship between the two, the cultural aspects of biomedicine, the ways in which the members of different cultures experience, understand and treat illness, and the transformation of traditional medical systems in the face of globalization.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ANTH
title: Perspectives on Discourse Tactics
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course examines cultural constructions of narrative and discourse. Topics include inter-tribal and inter-ethnic interaction; communication between genders and classes; discourse in institutional settings such as the courtroom, classroom and work settings; and discourse in the media.

subject: ANTH
title: Applied and Public Anthropology - Work Integrated Learning
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Other Hour(s): 2 Other Hours Schedule Type: Experiential Learning This course offers a critical study of public and practicing anthropology with a focus on how to apply anthropological skills and knowledge beyond the university. Central to this course will be an opportunity to apply anthropological skills in a work-integrated learning context such as with government departments or agencies, not-for-profit organizations, or private sector companies. Possible topics may include the application of anthropology in migration, globalization, Indigenous rights, human rights, health, disability, collections management, environment, cultural resource management, and museum studies.

subject: ANTH
title: Urban Anthropology
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course is a study of tribalism, ethnicity, sub-cultures, social networks and related phenomena in urban societies. Attention will be paid to planning and applied urban Anthropology.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ANTH
title: Special Topics in Biological Anthropology
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture The specific topics of this course will vary based on the expertise of the faculty members in biological anthropology.

subject: ANTH
title: Special Topics in Cultural Anthropology
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture The specific topics of this course will vary based on the expertise of the faculty members in cultural anthropology.

subject: ANTH
title: Special Topics in Archaeology
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture The specific topics of this course will vary based on the expertise of the faculty members in archaeology .

subject: ANTH
title: Forensic Anthropology
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture In this course, students will explore the role that forensic anthropologists play in medico-legal investigations, mass disasters, and human rights cases. Students will learn how to identify individualizing features and build a biological profile from skeletonized remains. The course will also examine topics such as forensic archaeology, time since death, taphonomy, and trauma.

subject: ANTH
title: Ancient Foodways
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course explores foodways through the scientific study of archaeological food remains. Using a multidisciplinary approach, students learn how humans adapted to their environment by gathering, hunting, raising, and growing food in the past. Topics include past human diets, systems of food production, and the role of sociopolitics and economics.

subject: ANTH
title: Directed Readings
description: Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Directed Reading Subject to the approval of the Chair. Directed Readings are intended to provide a more flexible approach for students who want to pursue and receive credit in areas of study which are of particular interest to them. Two Directed Reading courses can be used for graduation purposes but they must be in different disciplines. A Directed Reading cannot replicate an existing course. The objectives of the Directed Reading course(s) must be filed in the Office of the Registrar and will be made available to any institution requesting them for evaluation purposes. Arrangements for Directed Readings must be completed on or before the Add/ Drop deadline.

subject: ANTH
title: Anthropological Theory
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course follows the development of anthropological theory from the inception of Anthropology as an academic discipline in the nineteenth century to contemporary theoretical configurations. Emphasis will be placed on the development of specialized approaches within the discipline. Examples and case studies will come from archaeology, biological anthropology, cultural anthropology and linguistic anthropology.

subject: ANTH
title: Advanced Topics in Cultural Anthropology
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture The specific topics of this seminar will vary based on the specialties of the faculty in cultural anthropology.

subject: ANTH
title: Advanced Topics in Biological Anthropology
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture The specific topic of this seminar will vary with the specialties of the faculty, while focusing on a developing area of interest in biological anthropology.

subject: ANTH
title: Advanced Topics in Linguistic Anthropology
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture The specific topics of this seminar will vary based on the specialties of the faculty in linguistic anthropology.

subject: ANTH
title: Experiential Research in Advanced Bioarchaeology
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 In this experiential course, students will investigate, discuss, critique, and apply advanced biological anthropological techniques and methods to analyze archaeological skeletal and/or dental remains. Practical components may use actual archaeological remains.

subject: ANTH
title: Advanced Topics in Archaeology
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture The specific topic of this seminar-based course may vary with the specialties of the faculty in Anthropology and their research activities.

subject: ANTH
title: Honours Research Design
description: Students of Anthropology 5110, working under the one-on-one supervision of a full-time faculty member, will develop and publicly present a research proposal in anthropology. The proposed project plan will be carried out in the second required course for the Anthropology Honours stream, ANTH 5120 .

subject: ANTH
title: Honours Thesis
description: Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Independent Study Anthropology Honours students are required to demonstrate their mastery of anthropological knowledge, including method and theory, by completing an independent written research project, culminating in an honours thesis.
notes: These prerequisite courses must be successfully completed by or during the fall term of the student’s final year in the Anthropology Honours BA degree.

subject: ARTH
title: The History of Art: Prehistory to Renaissance
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course provides a critical overview of the visual arts from the Old Stone Age to the Renaissance. Students are offered a systematic study of aesthetics and the development of art styles which will enable them to develop a basic appreciation and understanding of art and its origins. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 2
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ARTH
title: The History of Western Art: Renaissance to the Present
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course provides a critical overview of the visual arts in Western Europe from the Renaissance to contemporary times. Recommended Preparation: ARTH 1101 GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 2
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ARTH
title: Hipsters, Tricksters and Adventurers
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course provides a chronological survey of the visual arts in Canada from 1870 to 1970. Topics may include: wilderness adventurers and voyageurs; Indigenous shamans and tricksters; modern hipsters and countercultural movements. Exploring a range of media and styles, classes offer in-depth knowledge of the varied themes and issues shaping Canadian art history. Recommended Preparation: Any 1000-level Art History course. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 2
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ARTH
title: Studies in Art, Design and Visual Culture
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course critically explores some of the significant themes in art, design, and visual culture. Themes such as art and power, class, revolution, and the body will be explored through a variety of sources. Artistic production, including architecture, painting and sculpture, and new media, will be examined to reveal thought-provoking connections across boundaries and time. Recommended Preparation: Any 1000-level Art History course. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 2
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ARTH
title: History of Modern Art from 1850 to 1920
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course is a critical look at the major schools, movements and themes of modern art in Europe and North America from 1850 to 1920. Recommended Preparation: Any 1000-level Art History course. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 2
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ARTH
title: Directed readings
description: Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Directed Reading Subject to the approval of the Chair. Directed Readings are intended to provide a more flexible approach for students who want to pursue and receive credit in areas of study which are of particular interest to them. Two Directed Reading courses can be used for graduation purposes but they must be in different disciplines. A Directed Reading cannot replicate an existing course. The objectives of the Directed Reading course(s) must be filed in the Office of the Registrar and will be made available to any institution requesting them for evaluation purposes. Arrangements for Directed Readings must be completed on or before the Add/ Drop deadline.

subject: ARTH
title: Modern Architecture
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course focuses on developments in architecture from the mid-nineteenth century to 1960. The course will examine new architectural forms that emerged during this time, brought about by profound social, cultural, political and technical transformations. Recommended Preparation: Any 1000-level Art History course. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 3
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ARTH
title: Art, Revolution and Pop Culture
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course examines modern art from 1920 to 1970 in the eras of the world wars, revolution and radicalism. Some of the topics covered include: surrealism and the avant-guarde; abstract painting and sculpture; pop culture and mass media; and public art and political space. Recommended Preparation: Any 1000-level Art History course. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 3
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ARTH
title: Impressionism: Art, Leisure & Society
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course will focus on the social history of Paris 1865-1885 as reflected in the subject matter of Impressionist art, particularly leisure. Issues such as industrialization, class, the secularization of society, the critic-dealer, and gender inequality also figure prominently. Recommended Preparation: Any 1000-level Art History course. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 3
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ARTH
title: Art & Ideas
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Art and Ideas investigates the discipline of art history through the exploration of influential art historical methodologies and theoretical perspectives that have emerged in the discipline. Recommended Preparation: Any 1000-level Art History course.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ARTH
title: Contemporary Art from 1970 to Today
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course examines Contemporary art from 1970 to the present in different geographic contexts focusing on various genres, themes and stylistic movements. In exploring the changing meaning of visual culture, the lectures analyze the theories, concepts and institutions informing current art practices. Recommended Preparation: Any 1000-level Art History Course. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 3
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ARTH
title: The Grand Tour: Travel Studies in Western Europe
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture The art and architecture of selected western European regions will be explored from an interdisciplinary perspective as a prelude to a field study trip to Europe, offering students an experiential knowledge and a greater appreciation of the regions’ artistic and cultural history.
notes: Students will be responsible for paying all costs, including travel, associated with the field trip.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ARTH
title: Travel Studies in Art and Architecture
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course is designed to promote a first-hand and focused engagement with art, architecture, and current exhibition practices. The locations of study will vary but will include at least one multi-night trip to destinations such as New York, Chicago, Los Angeles, Toronto and Montreal. Recommended Preparation: ARTH 1101 and/or ARTH 1103
notes: Students will be responsible for paying all costs, including travel, associated with the field trip. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 3 Students will be responsible for paying all costs, including travel, associated with the field trip. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 3

subject: ARTH
title: Directed readings
description: Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Directed Reading Subject to the approval of the Chair. Directed Readings are intended to provide a more flexible approach for students who want to pursue and receive credit in areas of study which are of particular interest to them. Two Directed Reading courses can be used for graduation purposes but they must be in different disciplines. A Directed Reading cannot replicate an existing course. The objectives of the Directed Reading course(s) must be filed in the Office of the Registrar and will be made available to any institution requesting them for evaluation purposes. Arrangements for Directed Readings must be completed on or before the Add/ Drop deadline.

subject: ARTH
title: Poetic Intentions in Architecture
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This advanced seminar examines significant buildings with the intention of discovering their essential nature. Students will explore architecture as the embodiment of ideas through the investigation of concepts ranging from light, transparency, opacity and texture, to more nuanced themes, such as the narrative of passage, extreme topographies and genius loci. The seminar provides opportunities for the exploration of links between theory and design in contemporary architecture.

subject: ARTH
title: Topics in Art History
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course offers the opportunity for advanced study of selected topics in art history. Specific content will vary by term and instructor.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ASTR
title: The Solar System
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This is primarily a descriptive course aimed at non-science students Conceptual understanding will be stressed over strict mathematical derivation. The topics covered in this course emphasize astronomy in our own solar neighbourhood. Topics include the Sun, the terrestrial and Jovian planets and their moons, comets, asteroids, as well as the formation of the solar system.
notes: Only one of ASTR 1101 and ASTR 1301 can be used for graduation purposes. This course cannot be used as a General Science Option​. GNED Cluster 1 - Tier 2 Only one of ASTR 1101 and ASTR 1301 can be used for graduation purposes. This course cannot be used as a General Science Option​. GNED Cluster 1 - Tier 2

subject: ASTR
title: The Universe at Large
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This is primarily a descriptive course aimed at non-sciencestudents. Conceptual understanding will be stressed over strict mathematical derivation. The topics covered in this course emphasize larger-scale astronomy. Topics include the lives and deaths of stars, supernovae, pulsars, black holes, the Milky Way and other galaxies, active galaxies and quasars, and cosmology.
notes: Only one of ASTR 1103 and ASTR 1303 can be used for graduation purposes. This course cannot be used as a General Science Option.​ GNED Cluster 1 - Tier 2 Only one of ASTR 1103 and ASTR 1303 can be used for graduation purposes. This course cannot be used as a General Science Option.​ GNED Cluster 1 - Tier 2

subject: ASTR
title: Planetary Astronomy
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course covers basic concepts of astronomy, with emphasis on our solar system. Topics include Newton’s laws and gravitation, the terrestrial and Jovian planets, comets, asteroids, the formation of the solar system, and extra-solar planets. This course stresses scientific explanation and uses mathematics, the language of science, whenever possible.
notes: Only one of ASTR 1101 and ASTR 1301 can be used for graduation purposes. GNED Cluster 1 - Tier 2, General Science Option

subject: ASTR
title: Stars, Galaxies, and Cosmology
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course covers basic concepts of astronomy, with emphasis on the universe outside the solar system. Topics include the properties of the Sun and other stars, the life and death of stars, galaxies, large-scale structure, and cosmology. This course stresses scientific explanation and uses mathematics, the language of science, whenever appropriate.
notes: Only one of ASTR 1103 and ASTR 1303 can be used for graduation purposes. GNED Cluster 1 - Tier 2, General Science Option

subject: ASTR
title: Astrobiology
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course will give an overview of the major topics in astrobiology. Topics include life on Earth, the conditions and possibility for life elsewhere in the solar system, the detection and properties of exoplanets, and SETI (Search for Extraterrestrial Intelligence). This course stresses scientific explanation and uses mathematics, the language of science.

subject: ASTR
title: Celestial Mechanics and Relativity
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course is an introduction to the subjects of celestial mechanics and special relativity, building on the prerequisites to develop a more advanced understanding of mechanics. It applies Newtonian mechanics to problems of interest in astronomy, such as planetary orbits, tidal effects and rigid body motion. It then goes on to investigate how Newtonian mechanics breaks down for large speeds and is replaced by special relativity.
notes: PHYS 2107 and ASTR 2107 are identical courses. Only one of these courses will count towards GPA calculation and graduation. General Science Option

subject: ASTR
title: Physical Cosmology
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This is an introductory course on physical cosmology, the branch of astronomy concerned with the origin of the universe and its subsequent evolution. The Big Bang Theory has become the standard theory of modern physical cosmology, and this course will develop the mathematical framework behind this theory and examine the constraints observations have placed on this theory. Typical topics to be covered include the Friedmann equation, the cosmic microwave background, the geometry of the universe, primordial nucleosynthesis, the cosmological constant, inflation, dark matter, and dark energy.

subject: ATTH
title: Professional Practice in Athletic Therapy
description: Lecture Hour(s): 2 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Other Hour(s): 3.25 Other Hours Schedule Type: Practicum This course is the first practicum for students majoring in Bachelor of Health and Physical Education - Athletic Therapy, which is comprised of two components. The first component is delivered with online content and focuses on specific theories related to professionalism within the Athletic Therapy profession as outlined by the Canadian Athletic Therapists Association Scope of Practice and Professional Competencies List. It will include subtopics of professionalism including but not limited to: communication skills, ethical practice, reliability/dependability, work ethic, compassion and problem-solving. Students must also complete an unpaid practicum where students shadow a Certified Athletic Therapist(s) for a minimum of 50 hours.
notes: Students must be prepared to attend clinical/field practicum anywhere in Calgary and must be open to options outside of Calgary. Community Service Learning
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ATTH
title: Clinical Musculoskeletal Anatomy
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course builds on the concepts taught in PHYL 1512 . The focus of this course is specific to regional musculoskeletal and neurological anatomy. In addition, this course will focus on surface anatomy and palpation skills, while integrating previous and current theoretical and practical knowledge in line with the Canadian Athletic Therapists Association Scope of Practice and Professional Competencies List.. Students will apply anatomical principles to range of motion and introduce the basics of functional movement. Practical aspects of this course will be integrated into lectures.

subject: ATTH
title: Prevention and Care of Athletic Injuries
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Lab Hour(s): 2 This course provides a study of the responsibilities of physical educators toward the basic principles of athletic therapy and their role in the prevention and treatment (first aid) of athletic injuries.

subject: ATTH
title: Musculoskeletal Assessment - Peripheral
description: Lecture Hour(s): 2 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Tutorial Hour(s): 2 This course involves assessment techniques for injuries and conditions associated with the upper limbs (finger, wrist, hand, forearm, elbow, upper arm, and shoulder) and the lower limbs (thigh, knee, lower leg, ankle, feet, and toes). Students will apply clinical reasoning techniques to generate a differential diagnosis that leads to a clinical impression or diagnosis. Students will demonstrate evidence-based, ethical, critical thinking, reflection, and collaborative practice while integrating previous and current theoretical and practical knowledge in line with the Canadian Athletic Therapists Association Scope of Practice and Professional Competencies List.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ATTH
title: Therapeutic Modalities
description: Lecture Hour(s): 2 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Tutorial Hour(s): 2 This course will provide theoretical and practical in-depth knowledge of the safe and effective use application of therapeutic clinical modalities (i.e. thermal agents, electrophysical agents). Some of the topics include: a review of the injury response process, pain theories, and the transmission and therapeutic application of electrical, thermal, acoustic, and mechanical energy, use of thermal agents, use of electrical agents and use of mechanical agents. These modalities are those typically used by Certified Athletic Therapists in Canada. Students will demonstrate evidence-based, ethical, critical thinking, reflection, and collaborative practice while integrating previous and current theoretical and practical knowledge in line with the Canadian Athletic Therapists Association Scope of Practice and Professional Competencies
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ATTH
title: Rehabilitation Techniques I
description: Lecture Hour(s): 2 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Tutorial Hour(s): 2 This course will provide theoretical and practical in-depth knowledge of the safe and effective use application of rehabilitation techniques to manage injuries and enhance the injury recovery of injury process. The techniques are those typically used by Certified Athletic Therapists in Canada. Students will demonstrate evidence-based, ethical, critical thinking, reflection, and collaborative practice while integrating previous and current theoretical and practical knowledge in line with the Canadian Athletic Therapists Association Scope of Practice and Professional Competencies List.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ATTH
title: Clinical Practicum I
description: Lecture Hour(s): 1 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Other Hour(s): 8 Other Hours Schedule Type: Practicum This is an introductory level clinical placement with an approved agency that focuses on clinical assessment and rehabilitation skills outlined in the Canadian Athletic Therapists Association Scope of Practice and Professional Competencies. Students will participate in lectures once every two weeks, complete 100 hours of practicum, and complete practicum and course requirements. Athletic Therapy students are expected to be open to working with diverse populations and gender identities across the lifespan.
notes: Students registered in ATTH 3150 should be aware that the start date of their practicum can begin prior to the start of the Fall semester. In addition, students are required to be available until the end of the final exam period in order to complete all practicum and course requirements. Students must be prepared to attend clinical/field practicum anywhere in Calgary and must be open to options outside of Calgary. Community Service Learning

subject: ATTH
title: Clinical Practicum II
description: Lecture Hour(s): 1 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Other Hour(s): 8 Other Hours Schedule Type: Practicum This is an intermediate-level clinical practicum with an approved agency that focuses on a higher level of responsibilities and expectations with clinical assessment and rehabilitation skills and an introduction to the application of rehabilitation skills. Students will demonstrate evidence-based, ethical, critical thinking, reflection, and collaborative practice while integrating previous and current theoretical and practical knowledge in line with the Canadian Athletic Therapists Association Scope of Practice and Professional Competencies List. Participants will participate in lectures once every two weeks, complete 100 hours of practicum, and complete practicum and course requirements. Athletic Therapy students are expected to be open to working with diverse populations and gender identities across the lifespan.
notes: Students registered in ATTH 3152 should be aware that the start date of their practicum can begin prior to the start of the Winter semester. In addition, students are required to be available until the end of the final exam period in order to complete all practicum and course requirements. Students must be prepared to attend clinical/field practicum anywhere in Calgary and must be open to options outside of Calgary. Community Service Learning
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: ATTH
title: Field Practicum I
description: Lecture Hour(s): 1 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Other Hour(s): 8 Other Hours Schedule Type: Practicum This is an introductory-level field placement with an approved agency that focuses on field evaluation and management skills. Students will demonstrate evidence-based, ethical, critical thinking, reflection, and collaborative practice while integrating previous and current theoretical and practical knowledge in line with the Canadian Athletic Therapists Association Scope of Practice and Professional Competencies List. Participants will participate in lectures once every two weeks, complete 100 hours of practicum, and complete practicum and course requirements. Athletic Therapy students are expected to be open to working with diverse populations and gender identities across the lifespan.
