The understanding of the course material will be further enhanced through exposure to both manual and computerized methods using current statistical computer software.

subject: MGMT
title: Creativity in the Workplace
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture In this course, students will gain an understanding of the concept of creativity-its nature, complexity, and relationship to major components present in any environment. They will analyze their own creative strengths and weaknesses, and will learn and practice tools to improve their creative competence. Through various learning activities, learners will develop an appreciation for why we need to learn about, and practice, creativity. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 2

subject: MGMT
title: Directed Readings
description: Lecture Hour(s): 4 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Directed Reading Subject to the approval of the Chair. Directed Readings are intended to provide a more flexible approach for students who want to pursue and receive credit in areas of study which are of particular interest to them. Two Directed Reading courses can be used for graduation purposes but they must be in different disciplines. A Directed Reading cannot replicate an existing course. The objectives of the Directed Reading course(s) must be filed in the Office of the Registrar and will be made available to any institution requesting them for evaluation purposes. Arrangements for Directed Readings must be completed on or before the Add/ Drop deadline.

subject: MGMT
title: Business Communication Theory and Practice
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course introduces students to the principles, strategies, and tools of professional business communication, emphasizing both written and verbal communication in workplace settings. Students will learn how to craft clear, concise, and audience-centered messages across multiple formats. By the end of the course, successful students will be equipped to communicate with clarity, confidence, and strategic intent, preparing them to navigate workplace interactions effectively and contribute meaningfully to professional environments.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: MGMT
title: Business Law
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Students will gain experience in legal problem solving with emphasis on legal issues involving business. The core of the course requires critical legal thinking in the following areas: agreements, liability, property rights, constitutional rights, and different ways to set-up businesses and non-profit activities. Additional topics will vary from semester to semester and may include crime, consumer protection, commercial transactions, creditor and debtor relations, and other timely legal topics.
notes: Only one of ENTR 3331 or MGMT 3230 can be used to satisfy program requirements. GNED Cluster 3 - Tier 3 Only one of ENTR 3331 or MGMT 3230 can be used to satisfy program requirements. GNED Cluster 3 - Tier 3

subject: MGMT
title: Management Information Systems
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course focuses on the role of information technology in an organization and its application to business problems. Techniques for designing, implementing and managing information systems are presented. Topics covered include hardware and software, file management, database organization, networking and telecommunications, systems development and design, flowcharting, system security, backup and recovery, and artificial intelligence.

subject: MGMT
title: Project Management
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course introduces students to project management Content includes concepts, principles, and the inputs, outputs, tools and techniques necessary to take the project from inception to completion. Students will learn, using PMBoK (Project Management Body of Knowledge), how to initiate a project, plan for its completion, monitor and control the project during implementation, and closing the completed project. Projects will be developed that are industry specific and will be used to allow students to take a project from inception to completion.

subject: MGMT
title: Organizational Behaviour
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course provides an examination of the behaviour of people in organizations. Emphasis will be placed on the application of theory through group interaction and case studies. Topics include: individual behaviour, group behaviour, leadership, motivation, conflict resolution, and organizational culture.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: MGMT
title: Business Integration
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This integrative course develops the applied skills associated with the implementation of business strategies across an organization. It focuses on evaluating the competitive environment and the internal resources that are associated with a small firm or a functional unit. From that understanding, students will explore how a manager or a divisional supervisor helps contribute to creating a competitive position for the firm. The course also helps students understand the integration of different organizational functions. Given the emphasis on the daily decisions of a manager, students will be able to understand and experience the challenges in running a company, through business-case discussions and an on-line simulation.

subject: MGMT
title: Directed Readings
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Directed Reading Subject to the approval of the Chair. Directed Readings are intended to provide a more flexible approach for students who want to pursue and receive credit in areas of study which are of particular interest to them. Two Directed Reading courses can be used for graduation purposes but they must be in different disciplines. A Directed Reading cannot replicate an existing course. The objectives of the Directed Reading course(s) must be filed in the Office of the Registrar and will be made available to any institution requesting them for evaluation purposes. Arrangements for Directed Readings must be completed on or before the Add/ Drop deadline.

subject: MGMT
title: Management Decision Analysis
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course provides an introduction to the concepts, methods and tools of logical, quantitative, and qualitative analyses to business decision-making and problem-solving under conditions of certainty, uncertainty, risk and competition. Both single person and group decision making are covered and applied problems from many areas of managerial decision making are considered. The course also covers topics such as behavioural economics, risk management and project decision-making, designed to explore the interpretation of results and the strengths and limitations of the various tools.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: MGMT
title: Data Management and Business Analytics
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Data, data and more data! This course focuses on data gathering, data management, and data governance within the context of business analytics. The course prepares students to uncover insights from structured and unstructured data sets to inform data-driven decision-making. At the end of the course, students will be able to work through the data life cycle, including pulling relevant data from multiple sources, organising the data, and using information technology to gather and interpret data. Students will also understand the fundamentals of ethical data governance and how to apply them in practice. They will have experience with relational databases and visual analytics.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: MGMT
title: Special Topics in Management
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course offers an in-depth exploration of current trends and key debates in management. The theme of the special topics course varies with each offering but will focus on a specific topic that is relevant to current business trends and issues. Topics may include shifting industry priorities, leadership, working within the broader social and environmental contexts, macroeconomic trends, firm-level issues, and team dynamics. Students will gain a comprehensive understanding of the selected topic through a combination of theoretical frameworks, practical applications, case studies, and discussions. As this is a variable content course, the specific topic will be announced each time the course is offered.

subject: MGMT
title: Advanced Business Law
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture From a broad based understanding of legal principles and problem solving gained in MGMT 3230 students will learn theory and develop skills in the following legal subjects: contract interpretation, drafting, and management; secured transaction and creditors rights; legal research and preparing briefs of the law; consumer protection; environmental protection; international business; and E-commerce and the law.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: MGMT
title: Corporate Social Responsibility
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course deals with the interrelationship of corporate social responsibility, corporate integrity, organizational culture, corporate strategy and organizational management/leadership. Topics covered may include: fair-trade and globalization and other converging and emerging ideas about the continuing evolution of CSR. Recommended Preparation: At least two courses from the Bissett School of Business that are 3000 level or higher. GNED Cluster 3 - Tier 3
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: MGMT
title: Environmental Management & Sustainable Development
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This is an advanced management course that introduces students to the issues, concepts, problems and processes of environmental management. Various management topics will be discussed including the design, implementation, and operation of an Environmental Management System, the tools of environmental management, and the concept of Sustainable Development. Students will gain an understanding of management processes and concepts and their application to the field of environmental management. Specific environmental issues will also be discussed and their relationship to the management of an organization. GNED Cluster 3 - Tier 3

subject: MGMT
title: Governance and Ethical Issues in Organizations
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Ethical governance in organizations can be described as the unnatural state. This course explores governance decision making in organizations. Emphasis will be placed on assessing the competing interests between social and personal values. Recommended Preparation: At least two courses at the 3000 level or higher. GNED Cluster 3 - Tier 3
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: MGMT
title: Leadership Development
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture The course offers a balanced integration of theory and practice of skills generally accepted as necessary to leaders of small enterprises and other organizations. The course is designed to help guide individuals in developing specific personal management competencies. Topics include problem solving, decision-making, team building, initiating and managing change, organization, and managing conflict.

subject: MGMT
title: Advanced Analytics in Business
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Building on previous courses, this Advanced Analytics course further develops students’ ability to run and meaningfully interpret descriptive and predictive analytics by introducing more advanced approaches to data analytics and data modeling strategies. Topics include providing an introduction to an advanced computing environment, model building with path analysis and/or structural equation modeling (including mediation and/or moderation analytical components), multiple regression with continuous or categorical outcomes and an introduction to machine learning.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: MGMT
title: Business Intelligence
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Building on previous courses, this course engages students in applying their analytics skills to develop and solve problems, and communicate the results through project based learning. In particular, students engage in identifying the organization question or objective to be addressed, identifying data relevant to solving the problem, exploring data to find trends, patterns and inconsistencies, and setting boundaries to know when the question is answered. Then they will apply their analytics skills to investigate the problem and communicate the results in multiple ways using accessible language.

subject: MGMT
title: Research Methods for Business
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course is an introduction to research methods for business. Students will gain an introduction to the philosophy of science, the research process (including theory development), and methods of primary and secondary data collection. Students will learn how to apply research methods to address a broad array of basic and applied research questions in the administrative sciences.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: MGMT
title: Honours Research Project
description: The BBA Honours requires students to complete an Honours Research Project in the form of either a thesis or applied research project with the support of a faculty supervisor. This project is an opportunity for students to integrate theory and practice and further their expertise in their field.
notes: Restricted to students officially admitted and registered in the Honours BBA Program.

subject: MGMT
title: Strategic Management
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This capstone course develops the conceptual and applied skills associated with analyzing a competitive situation from a general management point of view. It addresses issues affecting the fundamental direction of the firm, considers the formulation and implementation of strategy, focuses on the extent to which different aspects of the firm fit with key environmental forces, and discusses the organization’s ability to leverage its unique core competencies. The course views the organization holistically and, as such, goes beyond the mere integration of specific organizational functions.

subject: MGMT
title: Applied Analytics - Strategy and Professional Practice
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This capstone course has students apply their learning from previous courses to engage in analytics projects from conception to presentation (and everything in between). In partnership with organizations, students will work with clients to clarify business objectives, identify, gather and organize data from multiple sources, identify problems and opportunities through in-depth data analysis, and communicate insights back to the clients to help guide decisions. Through these experiential learning experiences, students will better understand data governance, ethical aspects of analytics, and the kinds of problems that can be solved through analytics.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: MKTG
title: Fundamentals of Marketing
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course provides an introduction to the fundamental concepts and principles of marketing including an introduction to the 4 P’s or marketing mix - product, price, place and promotion. Upon successful completion of this course, the student will have the ability to: evaluate customer wants and needs; analyze and interpret market research; link trends in the environment to marketing decisions; develop a customer or target market profile for a product (good, service or idea); and communicate a marketing strategy effectively in visual, verbal and written formats. This course serves as the foundation for subsequent marketing courses. GNED Cluster 3 - Tier 2
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: MKTG
title: Professional Selling
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course provides an in-depth study of the sales process: prospecting, presenting, meeting objections and closing. Emphasis is placed on understanding the sales process and developing the skills necessary for successful selling. Using information access and retrieval skills, a complete sales presentation is developed and presented to the class.

subject: MKTG
title: Directed Readings
description: Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Directed Reading Subject to the approval of the Chair. Directed Readings are intended to provide a more flexible approach for students who want to pursue and receive credit in areas of study which are of particular interest to them. Two Directed Reading courses can be used for graduation purposes but they must be in different disciplines. A Directed Reading cannot replicate an existing course. The objectives of the Directed Reading course(s) must be filed in the Office of the Registrar and will be made available to any institution requesting them for evaluation purposes. Arrangements for Directed Readings must be completed on or before the Add/ Drop deadline.

subject: MKTG
title: The Science of Persuasion
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Marketing ultimately hinges on the ability to comprehend human motivation in order to understand the attitudes and behaviours of people and organizations. In this course, students investigate the theory and practice of persuasion, learning to use these concepts to develop persuasive and compelling stories in visual, verbal and written formats.

subject: MKTG
title: Evidence-Based Marketing
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course explores the goals of evidence-based marketing and essential methodologies used to gain insight. Students will have the opportunity to learn how to support effective marketing decisions through focused market research and analysis. A key outcome of this course is the initial development of a student’s co-curricular professional portfolio and associated professional development plan.

subject: MKTG
title: Consumer Behaviour
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Consumer behaviour is a discipline that looks at why and how people acquire, consume, and dispose of the things they do. This marketing discipline applies knowledge from areas such as psychology, sociology, anthropology, and economics to understand how consumers are likely to behave. These insights are valuable to those interested in either marketing strategy or consumer welfare. A variety of situations will be analyzed with special reference to an individual, group, and cultural influences.

subject: MKTG
title: Fundamentals of Sports and Entertainment Marketing
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This is a specialized course for students interested in expanding their knowledge of the sports and entertainment industry as a business and economic force. It will provide students with a framework for understanding the scope of the sports and entertainment business across the various leagues, the venues, the sponsors, the media companies, the agencies, the athletes and artists and their relationships to internal and external factors, and the marketing applications that drive this complex and growing multi-billion-dollar industry. The sports and entertainment business has become a persistent and integral part of our economy, specifically in the multi-media and entertainment arenas. The sports and entertainment business has grown significantly and is highly competitive for fans, airtime, media coverage and revenues, and sponsorship dollars. This has made managing events, venues, artists, and athletes, as well as the accountability of sponsorship and celebrity endorsement, a high priority with bottom-line relevance.
notes: This highly experiential course is primarily taught in the offices and facilities of sports and entertainment organizations in downtown Calgary. If you choose to take a course at Mount Royal on the same day, you must leave sufficient travel time between locations. Community Service Learning

subject: MKTG
title: Directed Readings
description: Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Directed Reading Subject to the approval of the Chair. Directed Readings are intended to provide a more flexible approach for students who want to pursue and receive credit in areas of study which are of particular interest to them. Two Directed Reading courses can be used for graduation purposes but they must be in different disciplines. A Directed Reading cannot replicate an existing course. The objectives of the Directed Reading course(s) must be filed in the Office of the Registrar and will be made available to any institution requesting them for evaluation purposes. Arrangements for Directed Readings must be completed on or before the Add/ Drop deadline.

subject: MKTG
title: Designing Impactful Brands
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course introduces the creative tools and processes essential to marketing. We will examine the fundamentals of design for brands and campaigns across media platforms. Students will have the opportunity of discovering methods to develop beautiful, well-informed creative solutions that meet an organization’s needs and resonate with its audiences.

subject: MKTG
title: Managing Marketing Relationships
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Business success depends on relationships with diverse groups of people, including customers, employees, shareholders, suppliers, distribution partners, communities and regulators. In this course students will have the opportunity to learn to identify, evaluate and prioritize these relationships. This will include areas of study such as social exchange theory, market segmentation and network analysis.

subject: MKTG
title: The Fundamentals of Branding
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture What are brands? Why do they matter to consumers and organizations? How do companies build strong brands for success in a competitive marketplace? Successful brand managers understand the interconnected perspectives of the consumer, the creative designer, and the management strategist. In this course, students will explore different dimensions of consumer-brand behaviour, the role of marketing communications and design in building brand equity, and what this means for strategic brand management.

subject: MKTG
title: Marketing Analytics and Insights
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture The marketer’s ability to understand complex issues today and anticipate a change in the future drives competitive advantage. In this course, students will have the opportunity to understand how to determine the relevant contextual variables that impact strategies, such as the size and culture of the business, the nature of the industry, the competitive dynamics, and the relevant political, legal, social and technological trends.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: MKTG
title: Design and Brand Research in Practice
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Other Hour(s): 12 Other Hours Schedule Type: Experiential Learning Design researchers collaborate across development, design, and marketing teams to evaluate current and upcoming user research needs that help to improve product definition and drive business goals. In this project-based course, students will learn how to create a research plan, find participants and integrate research methods in the context of a specific branding project. Some of the topics explored will be qualitative, quantitative, online and ethnographic interview techniques, video and photo documentary, immersion, participant-aided data gathering, prototype-assisted observation, methods for organizing data, finding patterns and distilling insights that lead to actionable and inspiring design directives for the brands of the future. We will also explore ways to integrate market research and design research to create insightful and impactful branded experiences, focusing on how their combined strengths can enhance design outcomes.

subject: MKTG
title: Special Topics in Marketing
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course provides an examination of selected topics in Marketing. As this is a variable content course, the specific topic will be announced and advertised each time it is offered.

subject: MKTG
title: Digital Marketing Studio
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course examines current trends in digital marketing. Students will learn how to strategically use a variety of digital marketing tools and tactics to capture consumers’ attention and achieve marketing goals. This course will explore a variety of topics such as content marketing, search engine optimization, email marketing, paid search, etc. as part of a larger digital marketing strategy.

subject: MKTG
title: Professional Brand Strategy
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture In today’s hyper-connected world, consumers expect more than just a good product or service: they demand a seamless and personalized brand experience that engages them at every touchpoint. How can brand leaders incite the conversations, collaborate in the experience creation, and build the engaging relationships they need to connect with their consumer community? This course is a hands-on, project-based studio experience that will bring together best practices in brand management, brand leadership and brand strategy to collaboratively and creatively explore these questions. Assignments will push student teams to audit a brand, focus on a strategic goal, and design a brand campaign that invites a targeted community to participate in a series of extraordinary personal experiences for a real life client. Teams will capture their learning in a comprehensive brand strategy report and campaign guide which they will pitch to their client in a live showcase. This course will integrate industry leading brand development practices, including design thinking, storytelling fundamentals, participatory design research, rapid prototyping, and a bias towards action.

subject: MKTG
title: Professional Marketing Strategy
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This senior course focuses on synthesizing the analytical marketing skills built through the degree program. In this course, students will have the opportunity to develop a comprehensive marketing strategy and translate this strategy into a concise marketing plan. A key outcome of this course is the submission and review of a student’s completed marketing portfolio.

subject: NTSC
title: An Introduction to Current Environmental Issues
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course involves a current, comprehensive and holistic overview of critical environmental issues for non-science majors. The environmental issues explored in this course from various points of view will serve as a valuable and solid foundation for students, who will be making decisions that shape the future of their society.
notes: This course cannot be used as a General Science Option. GNED Cluster 1 - Tier 2 This course cannot be used as a General Science Option. GNED Cluster 1 - Tier 2

subject: NTSC
title: An Introduction to Forensic Science
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course offers students an opportunity to gain an understanding of a variety of forensic techniques, ranging from blood pattern analysis and identification of human remains, to gas chromatography, and DNA ‘fingerprinting’. Successful students will also gain insight into how the ‘CSI Effect’ has altered the relationship between science and the legal system.
notes: This course cannot be used as a General Science Option. GNED Cluster 1 - Tier 2 This course cannot be used as a General Science Option. GNED Cluster 1 - Tier 2

subject: NTSC
title: Planet Earth and Society
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture An examination of human societies’ growing need for Earth’s materials, energy, food, and water-coupled with environmental and social implications of resource and energy use. Balancing environmental sustainability with the resource extraction from Earth that is necessary for society is one of the greatest challenges facing our planet and the future of humanity. Issues discussed include geological processes and the resources they produce; population and resource consumption; effects of human activities on natural systems; global cycles in the context of their impact on society; and strategies for promoting sustainability. Learning activities are guided by case studies, group discussions and term projects.
notes: This course can not be used as a General Science Option. GNED Cluster 3 - Tier 2

subject: NTSC
title: Forensic Science I: Lab Analysis by Specialists
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Lab Hour(s): 3 This course deals with the theoretical and practical aspects of forensics crime scene analysis. Topics will include: the discovery and examination of evidence found in and around human/animal remains to determine time of death; the analysis of drugs, unknown powders, bodily fluids found at crime scenes; determination of document fraud and arson; discussions about the use of computers and databases to help solve crimes and pinpoint suspects. A discussion of the legal and ethical issues resulting from the use of forensics scientific analysis and conduct in forensic investigations will also be included.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: NTSC
title: Environmentally Sustainable and Healthy Societies
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Every human being, every institution and every organization can make a difference in the quest for a sustainable and healthy future for humankind. This course will examine current practices that result in unhealthy societies and nonsustainable environmental impacts and the changes that must be made in order to establish a sustainable and healthy future planet. Our future depends on managing our planet in ways that create sustainable relationships with our ecosystems.
notes: This course cannot be used as a General Science Option. GNED Cluster 1 - Tier 2 This course cannot be used as a General Science Option. GNED Cluster 1 - Tier 2

subject: NTSC
title: Experimental Science for Elementary and Junior High Education
description: Lecture Hour(s): 2 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Lab Hour(s): 2 This course will provide science-teaching and curricular theory along with practical examples of material and demonstrations that can be used in a grade-school classroom. This course is a survey of experimental content and methods from all disciplines and levels within the Alberta Education Elementary and Junior High School Programs of Study. Students will be expected to participate in activities, write up lab reports, and design, test, and present their own demonstration to the class.
notes: This course cannot be used as a General Science Option.

subject: NTSC
title: Environmental Health
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Many aspects of human health are affected by the environment, and many diseases can be initiated, promoted, sustained or stimulated by environmental factors. For that reason the interactions of people with their environment are an important component of public health. This course introduces students to the complexity of environmental health issues facing society today.
notes: This course cannot be used as a General Science Option. GNED Cluster 1 - Tier 3

subject: NTSC
title: Environmental and Developmental Legacy of Alberta’s Oil and Gas Industry
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course investigates the development of Alberta’s oil and gas industry in relation to how it affects our environment and our socio-economic development. Topics covered include analyses of changes in the environmental and socioeconomic status of Alberta since the oil discovery; and how changes in petroleum technologies and petroleum policies affect our environment and our economy. These concepts will be illustrated by using case studies drawn from companies and agencies involved in the Alberta’s oil and gas development.
notes: This course cannot be used as a General Science Option. This course cannot be used as a General Science Option.

subject: NTST
title: Introduction to Native Studies
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course will provide an overview of Aboriginal world views and belief systems. Students will gain an awareness of similarities and differences among First Nations in the area of social organizations, spiritual beliefs and cultural practices.

subject: NURS
title: Theoretical Foundations of Nursing I
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course situates Nursing within Indigenous Teachings from respected Elders and Knowledge Keepers as the foundation for the Bachelor of Nursing Program. Understanding health is a human right is foundational to support the provision of just, equitable practice. NURS 1111 gives students an introduction to a variety of knowledge(s), skills, attitudes and discernment in the profession of nursing in the context of multidimensional and dynamic environments.

subject: NURS
title: Health Promotion with Individuals I
description: Lecture Hour(s): 2 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Lab Hour(s): 2 This course is focused on an individual’s holistic experience of health within the diversity of family, community, health, and professional resources to support and promote health and health equity. Within a lens of relational practice (based on Indigenous Teachings from respected Elders and Knowledge Keepers), students will integrate theory and practice holistic health assessments related to determinants and patterns of health, foundational care principles, interventions, and health promotion strategies.

subject: NURS
title: Health Promotion with Individuals II
description: Lecture Hour(s): 2 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Lab Hour(s): 2 This course is a continuation of the Health Promotion for Individuals I. Additional focus will be placed on understanding the individual’s holistic experience of health within the context of family, community, health and professional resources to support and promote health and health equity. Within a lens of relational practice (based on Indigenous Teachings from respected Elders and Knowledge Keepers) students continue to integrate theory and will practice holistic health assessments related to the determinants and patterns of health, directing nursing care and health promotion interventions.
notes: This course builds on content from other 1000 level Midwifery courses. Students are recommended to take courses in 1000 level of the program prior to registering for this course.

subject: NURS
title: Professional Practice I
description: Other Hour(s): 8 Other Hours Schedule Type: Clinical This practicum course has a focus on promoting holistic health and providing safe, strengths-based and evidence-based nursing care to diverse individuals experiencing alterations in health. Using relational practice, students will integrate understandings of patterns of health, the context of the determinants of health, and the principles of primary health care in practiced based settings.
notes: Students must be prepared to attend clinical anywhere in Calgary and be open to options outside of Calgary.

subject: NURS
title: Theoretical Foundations of Nursing II
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course continues to expand on nursing science from qualitative, affective, relational, strengths-based theory and concepts in the context of the adult facing chronic health challenges in the tertiary care setting. Students will continue to develop a broad range of leadership and academic skills and gain a more holistic understanding of the human health experience in the context of family within local acute care settings. An intersectional analysis approach in this course focuses on the client-nurse relationship, with the aim to deconstruct historical (through to current) issues in the intersectoral domains of nursing practice: clinical, education, research, and administration.

subject: NURS
title: Pathophysiology: Foundations for Nursing Practice
description: Lecture Hour(s): 4 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Lab Hour(s): 2 This second-year course is designed to deepen students’ understanding of health and illness across diverse populations, with a strong emphasis on the determinants of health and primary health care principles. Through a combination of academic study and simulated practice, students will develop the knowledge, skills, and judgment required to provide safe, ethical, and evidence-based nursing care to individuals, families, communities, and population groups. Students will explore the impact of health alterations on people and their environments, applying critical thinking and caring inquiry to assess, plan and evaluate nursing interventions. The course fosters collaborative partnerships and introduces advocacy and capacity-building strategies to support health promotion and illness prevention. By the end of the course, students will demonstrate competence in integrating theoretical knowledge with clinical practice, interpreting complex health scenarios, and making informed recommendations for nursing care in both simulated and real-world settings.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: NURS
title: Pharmacology: Nursing Knowledge and Application
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Lab Hour(s): 2 This second-year course builds on students’ pharmacological knowledge to support safe, evidence-based nuring care across diverse populations. Emphasizing primary health care and social determinants, this course combines academic study with simulated practice to develop clinical judgment in medication management. Students will examine how pharmocotherapuetics addresses health alterations, applying critical thinking to assess, plan and evaluate medication regimens. NURS 2113 emphasizes collaborative care, patient education and advocacy strategies to promote medication adherence, optimize therapeutic outcomes and prevent adverse drug events. By the end of the course, students will demonstrate competence in integrating pharmacological theroy with clinical decision-making, interpreting complex medication-related scenarios and making informed recommendations for drug therapy in both simulated and real-world settings.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: NURS
title: Professional Practice II
description: Other Hour(s): 16 Other Hours Schedule Type: Clinical This course focuses on promoting the health of individuals from and within diverse contexts of care using a strengths-based approach and considering the indiviudual’s position within both their family and community. Students will use critical thinking and clinical judgment skills in the application of pharmacology, pathophysiology, nursing science and knowledge, nursing standards of care and professional structures and accountability toward holistic health outcomes within a relational approach to care while engaging with other members of the health care team in the planning and provision of nursing care of adults experiencing alterations in health in hospital settings.
notes: Students must be prepared to attend clinical anywhere in Calgary and be open to options outside of Calgary.

subject: NURS
title: Health Promotion with Populations, Communities and Families
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course focuses on the principles of population/public health, primary health care, health promotion and the determinants of health as a means of understanding how to impact the health of communities and populations and the larger social, political, cultural and economic factors and intersections that impact health. This course will focus on social justice and health equity at the community and population levels. Students will learn how to interpret theoretical knowledge in order to make recommendations for nursing practice at the community and population levels.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: NURS
title: Professional Practice III
description: Other Hour(s): 11 Other Hours Schedule Type: Clinical This course will provide opportunities for students to use various population health and community health models while working with diverse underserved population groups across the lifespan. Students will work within various phases of the community health process including community health assessment, analysis, diagnosis, intervention and evaluation. Students complete a population/aggregate assessment and develop planned intervention considering the principles of primary health care, social and structural determinants of health, and population health within a community setting. Students will integrate previous and current theoretical and practical knowledge.
notes: Students must be prepared to attend clinical anywhere in Calgary and be open to options outside of Calgary. Community Service Learning

subject: NURS
title: Adult Health
description: Lecture Hour(s): 6 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture In this course, students will use a relational, strengths-based approach, to consider population groups and influences, as a framework to promote health, and provide holistic and equitable care for a diversity of adults and families who experience complex acute and chronic illness. Students will engage in care coordination within a collaborative environment to consider inter-professional partnerships, critical thinking, advocacy, trauma informed care, to build capacity, and clinical judgment in a variety of contexts.
notes: It is recommended that students complete NURS 3102 and NURS 3104 concurrently to best support the integration of theory and clinical practice.

subject: NURS
title: Professional Practice in Adult Health
description: Other Hour(s): 24 Other Hours Schedule Type: Clinical Within NURS 3104 and in a variety of settings and contexts, students will focus on the integration of primary health care and population health strategies within a relational lens to promote health and provide care to a diverse and inclusive adult population and families experiencing complex acute and chronic illness in a variety of settings and contexts. Using relational practice, students will demonstrate evidence-informed, ethical, safe, and effective nursing care to demonstrate critical thinking, clinical judgment, and collaboration with a focus on the integration of previous and current theoretical and practical knowledge.
notes: It is recommended that students complete NURS 3102 and NURS 3104 concurrently to best support the integration of theory and clinical practice. Students must be prepared to attend clinical anywhere in Calgary and be open to options outside of Calgary.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: NURS
title: Family Newborn Health
description: Lecture Hour(s): 6 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture In this course, students will use a relational, strengths-based approach with childbearing individuals and families to promote health, and provide holistic and equitable care. Students will engage and collaborate with families to promote health and consider inter-professional partnerships, critical thinking, advocacy, family centered practices, and clinical judgment in a variety of contexts.
notes: It is recommended that students complete NURS 3112 and NURS 3114 concurrently to best support the integration of theory and clinical practice.

subject: NURS
title: Professional Practice in Family Newborn Health
description: Other Hour(s): 24 Other Hours Schedule Type: Clinical This practicum course has a focus on the integration of primary health care and population health strategies using a strength-based approach to care for childbearing individuals and families in a variety of settings and contexts. Using relational practice, students will demonstrate evidence-based, ethical, safe, and effective nursing care to demonstrate critical thinking, clinical judgment, and collaboration with a focus on the integration of previous and current theoretical and practical knowledge.
notes: It is recommended that students complete NURS 3112 and NURS 3114 concurrently to best support the integration of theory and clinical practice. Students must be prepared to attend clinical anywhere in Calgary and be open to options outside of Calgary.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: NURS
title: Child Health
description: Lecture Hour(s): 6 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture In this course, students will use a relational, strengths-based approach, to consider population groups and influences, as a framework to promote health and equitable care for a diversity of children and families considering their unique needs. Students will engage in care coordination within a collaborative environment to consider inter-professional partnerships, critical thinking, advocacy, trauma informed care, to build capacity, and clinical judgment in a variety of contexts.
notes: It is recommended that students complete NURS 3122 and NURS 3124 concurrently to best support the integration of theory and clinical practice.

subject: NURS
title: Professional Practice in Child Health
description: Other Hour(s): 24 Other Hours Schedule Type: Clinical In a variety of settings and contexts, students will focus on the integration of primary health care and population health strategies within a relational lens to promote health and provide care to diverse and inclusive children and families. Using relational practice, students will demonstrate evidence-informed, ethical, safe, and effective nursing care to demonstrate critical thinking, clinical judgment, and collaboration with a focus on the integration of previous and current theoretical and practical knowledge.
notes: It is recommended that students complete NURS 3122 and NURS 3124 concurrently to best support the integration of theory and clinical practice. Students must be prepared to attend clinical anywhere in Calgary and be open to options outside of Calgary. Community Service Learning
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: NURS
title: Mental Health
description: Lecture Hour(s): 6 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture In this course, students will use a relational, strengths-based approach, to consider population groups and influences, as a framework to promote health, and provide holistic and equitable care for a diversity of adults and families who experience acute and chronic mental illness. Students will engage in care coordination within a collaborative environment to consider inter-professional partnerships, critical thinking, advocacy, trauma informed care, to build capacity, and clinical judgment in a variety of contexts.
notes: It is recommended that students complete NURS 3132 and NURS 3134 concurrently to best support the integration of theory and clinical practice.

subject: NURS
title: Professional Practice in Mental Health
description: Other Hour(s): 24 Other Hours Schedule Type: Clinical Students will focus on the integration of primary health care and population health strategies within a relational lens to promote health and provide care to a diverse and inclusive adult population and families experiencing acute and chronic mental health concerns in a variety of settings and contexts. Using relational practice, students will demonstrate evidence-informed, ethical, safe, and effective nursing care to demonstrate critical thinking, clinical judgment, and collaboration with a focus on the integration of previous and current theoretical and practical knowledge.
notes: It is recommended that students complete NURS 3132 and NURS 3134 concurrently to best support the integration of theory and clinical practice. Students must be prepared to attend clinical anywhere in Calgary and be open to options outside of Calgary.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: NURS
title: Gerontological Nursing
description: Lecture Hour(s): 6 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture In this course, students will utilize gerontological nursing knowledge and skills in a relational care approach to promote health and well-being, foster resilience and adaptation to change, optimize function, and prevent illness and injury of diverse older adults and their care partners. Students will learn the concepts of age-related changes, ageism, theories of aging, interprofessional partnerships, chronic disease management, and life transitions to understand the complexities of aging within the healthcare environment.
notes: It is recommended that students complete NURS 3142 and NURS 3144 concurrently to best support the integration of theory and clinical practice.

subject: NURS
title: Professional Practice in Gerontological Nursing
description: Other Hour(s): 24 Other Hours Schedule Type: Clinical In various settings and contexts, students will use a life course perspective to apply gerontological nursing skills and knowledge in a strengths-based approach to caring for older persons and their care partners. Using relational practice, students will demonstrate evidence-informed, ethical, safe, and effective nursing care to demonstrate critical thinking, clinical judgment, and collaboration with a focus on the integration of previous and current theoretical and practical knowledge.
notes: It is recommended that students complete NURS 3142 and NURS 3144 concurrently to best support the integration of theory and clinical practice. Students must be prepared to attend clinical anywhere in Calgary and be open to options outside of Calgary.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: NURS
title: Trends and Issues in Nursing Leadership
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture In this course, students will examine concepts of leadership and critically appraise the changing social, geopolitical and health care environments that influence the nursing profession, nursing practice and health. Students will develop appropriate strategies to strengthen their capacity to influence and respond to current trends and issues.

subject: NURS
title: Integrated Professional Practice
description: Other Hour(s): 30 Other Hours Schedule Type: Clinical This practicum course is a consolidated experience in which students apply health promotion, principles of primary health care, and leadership to promote holistic health. Using relational practice, students will integrate clinical judgement, caring inquiry, collaborative practice, and reflection while integrating previous and concurrent theoretical and practical knowledge. This course assists students to prepare for the transition to increasingly independent practice in the fourth-year nursing course and the transition to independent practice.
notes: Students must be prepared to attend clinical anywhere in Calgary and be open to options outside of Calgary.

subject: NURS
title: Team Approaches to Chronic Disease Management
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Students will explore chronic disease globally, nationally and locally, using knowledge of pharmacology, pathophysiology, determinants of health and relational nursing practice. Students will develop collaborative management plans for diverse individuals experiencing chronic illness and will appraise and justify their roles as independent practitioners within the interdisciplinary team.

subject: NURS
title: Family Nursing: A Contextual Practice
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course highlights the context of family life and health throughout the lifespan. Building on previous knowledge, students will consider how systematic family theories inform interventions in lifespan development while working with strengths and challenges in family processes within the current geo-social and political context of families.

subject: NURS
title: A Palliative Approach for Individuals and Families
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Learners will integrate and apply their previous knowledge and experience in a new context: hospice palliative care. Learners will examine family-centred/goal-driven approaches to palliation, considering collaborative practice, inter-professional partnerships and their own clinical reasoning in palliation contexts.

subject: NURS
title: Diversity in Health
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course provides a foundation for working with diverse populations locally, nationally and internationally by examining the underpinnings of diversity, inclusiveness and cultural attunement. Learners will examine how diversity can affect health and aid them in recognition of bias. They will gain an appreciation of diversity within individuals and populations.

subject: NURS
title: Influencing Health, Equity and Social Action
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Students will use a variety of learning strategies to create and shape change through influencing social, geo-political and policy agendas. Learners will apply sociopolitical advocacy skills and transformative leadership to address the root causes of health inequities in order to improve the health of individuals, families, communities and populations.

subject: NURS
title: Awareness, Resilience, Advocacy, and Action
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course provides future healthcare providers with a greater understanding and the ability to develop skills to navigate current health disparities and the realities of healthcare in Canada. Treaty 7 Elders, Knowledge Keepers, and Educators were integral in the development and facilitation of this course in order to provide wisdom and insight into the awareness of Indigenous experience, opportunities for understanding Indigenous resilience, explore the potential for advocacy and to help deconstruct the systematic and personal barriers through social action. Classes will be offered by both Indigenous and Non-Indigenous Educators, and the mode delivery will focus on oral discussions and experiential stories. At the completion of the course, future healthcare professional will have greater skill in advocating and supporting Indigenous Communities within a framework of respect, empowerment and resilience

subject: NURS
title: Supportive Environments For Mental Health
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Other Hour(s): 3 This fully online course is oriented around the question, How can approaches to mental health and health promotion shape a supportive environment? The goal will be to explore the struggles and tensions of approaches that dominate current mental health promotion. This course takes as a starting point the fact that we live in a world of tensions that are worthy of exploration and critical thought. Specific tensions in mental health promotion we will be examining in this course are the approaches of: Problem Solving and Empowerment, Integration and Inclusion, and Protection and Liberation. We will engage in complex but important questions and ask students to understand, examine and critically review the various and differing approaches to mental health at multiple levels of intervention.

subject: NURS
title: Transition to Independent Practice
description: Other Hour(s): 28.5 Other Hours Schedule Type: Clinical This precepted experience provides an opportunity for students to demonstrate a full scope of professional nursing practice. Students will care for selected persons (individual, family, community, population) in a variety of nursing practice settings, using health promotion, primary health care and population health strategies. Students will exemplify evidence-based, ethical, safe and effective nursing care, and demonstrate critical thinking, caring inquiry, reflection, autonomy and collaborative practice in preparation for graduation. It is expected that the student will integrate all previous theoretical and practical knowledge. This 5000 level course builds on the content in the previous levels of the program. Students must complete all Nursing courses in the lower levels of the program prior to registering for this course.
notes: Restricted to students currently enrolled in the 4th year of the Bachelor of Nursing program. Other students need approval of department Chair. Students must be prepared to attend clinical anywhere in Calgary and be open to options outside of Calgary.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: PHED
title: Water Safety Instructor
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course includes a comprehensive study of the techniques of teaching swimming, water safety and life saving. Other aquatic activities such as aqua aerobics, diving, competitive swimming and snorkeling will be introduced. Students must demonstrate competent swimming, rescue and artificial respiration skills.
notes: Students will be responsible for payment of fees to outside organizations when the issue of a certificate involves a fee.

subject: PHED
title: Clinical Practicum I
description: Lecture Hour(s): 2 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Other Hour(s): 13 Other Hours Schedule Type: Practicum This is a formal work experience with an approved agency. Participants will be involved in integration seminars, and will be required to participate in the specified hours of practicum and complete the assignments as required. Typical assignments are log book recordings of activities and case study analysis of situations specific to fieldwork of a Certified Athletic Therapist in Canada. This work experience is specific and under the supervision of a paramedical professional such as a Certified Athletic Therapist, physiotherapist, sports medicine physician, chiropractor or sports massage therapist. Please note that the code of ethics for each of those professionals must be investigated and adhered to.
notes: This course will be delivered in a block placement format. This course is limited to students enroled in the Advanced Certificate in Athletic Therapy who have successfully completed or are concurrently enroled in the core course requirements.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: PHED
title: Advanced Clinical and Field Practicum
description: Other Hour(s): 21 Other Hours Schedule Type: Clinical This is a formal work experience with an approved agency. Participants will be involved in integration seminars, and will be required to participate in the specified hours of practicum and complete the assignments as required. Typical assignments are logbook recordings of activities and case study analyses of situations specific to field and clinical work of a Certified Athletic Therapist in Canada. This work experience is specific and under the supervision of a paramedical professional such as a Certified Athletic Therapist, physiotherapist, sports medicine physician, chiropractor or sports massage therapist.

subject: PHIL
title: Philosophy: Knowledge and Existence
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Tutorial Hour(s): 1 This course provides an introduction to philosophy through the discussion of selected topics such as: skepticism, perception, personal identity, free will and determinism and God. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 2
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: PHIL
title: Philosophy: Sex and Love
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Tutorial Hour(s): 1 This course provides an introduction to philosophy through an exploration of philosophical questions about sex and love. GNED Cluster 3 - Tier 2
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: PHIL
title: Philosophy: Values and Ethics
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Tutorial Hour(s): 1 This course provides an introduction to philosophy through a first enquiry into the nature and justification of moral standards. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 2
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: PHIL
title: Critical Thinking
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Tutorial Hour(s): 1 This course offers an introduction to deductive and inductive techniques used in appraising arguments. The course may contain some elementary formal logic, but its main focus will be on analyzing arguments as they occur in everyday life and ordinary language. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 2

subject: PHIL
title: Introduction to Symbolic Logic
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Tutorial Hour(s): 1 This course introduces sentential and first-order logic from both deductive and semantic points of view. Some elementary metatheorems are also discussed. GNED Cluster 1 - Tier 2

subject: PHIL
title: Medieval Philosophy
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture The Medieval Period covers philosophical developments from the Roman Empire to the Renaissance. This course will investigate a subset of topics from this period selected from such things as moral and political philosophy, logic and metaphysics, epistemology, and philosophical theology. Included may be representative works from Latin, Arabic and Hebraic thinkers. Recommended Preparation: Any Philosophy course. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 2
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: PHIL
title: The Classical Period
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture An introduction to philosophy through the study of a period in its history. A selection of philosophers from the Pre-Socratics, through Plato and Aristotle, to the Hellenistic and Roman philosophers will be discussed. Recommended Preparation: Any Philosophy course. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 3
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: PHIL
title: The Enlightenment Philosophers
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course is an introduction to philosophy through the study of a period in its history. A selection of philosophers from Descartes through Kant will be discussed. Recommended Preparation: Any Philosophy course. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 3
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: PHIL
title: Nineteenth Century Philosophy
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course serves as an introduction to nineteenth-century philosophy, including figures like Schiller, Fichte, Hegel, Darwin, Kierkegaard, Marx, Peirce, Nietzsche, Freud, and Bergson. Students will explore debates about the nature of life, history, rationality, freedom, and religion, becoming familiar with a range of philosophical approaches. Recommended Preparation: Any Philosophy course. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 2
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: PHIL
title: Philosophy of Law
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture An investigation of philosophical accounts of the nature of law and legal systems. Acquaints the students with central positions in jurisprudence, such as the nature of law, the relationship of law to morality, punishment, rights, and justice. Recommended Preparation: Any Philosophy course. GNED Cluster 3 - Tier 3
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: PHIL
title: Metaphysics
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture An examination of such topics as categories, existence, persons, space, time, and necessity. Recommended Preparation: Any Philosophy course. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 3
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: PHIL
title: Bio Ethics
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture A critical and analytical examination of ethical and legal problems arising in and about health care. Issues to be considered may include: euthanasia, abortion, the conditions for the withdrawal of treatment, the physician-patient relationship, research on human subjects, and genetic engineering. The practical applications of ethical and legal theory are emphasized. Recommended Preparation: Any Philosophy course. GNED Cluster 3 - Tier 3
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: PHIL
title: Business Ethics
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture A critical and analytical examination of some central moral problems that arise in and for business. Emphasis throughout the course will be placed not only on the details of the particular problems studied but also on the conceptual and other tools needed to understand and resolve or solve such problems. Topics to be discussed will include: the moral responsibilities and rights of corporations and their officers, codes of business ethics, and conflicts of responsibilities and rights. Recommended Preparation: Any Philosophy course. GNED Cluster 3 - Tier 3
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: PHIL
title: Feminist Philosophy
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Issues in feminist philosophy and methodology. Topics may include feminist theories of knowledge and science, ethics, metaphysics, political theory and feminist methodology. Recommended Preparation: Any Philosophy course. GNED Cluster 3 - Tier 3
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: PHIL
title: Existentialism
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Existentialist thought is a historical movement in philosophy. It is also a methodology of philosophy that explores and demonstrates the significance of human existence itself. This course serves as an introduction to existentialist methods, debates, and concepts, as well as to significant existentialist thinkers. Recommended Preparation: Any Philosophy course. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 2
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: PHIL
title: Social and Political Philosophy
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture In this course some of the fundamental issues in social and political thought are studied. Issues to be studied may include rights, justice, authority, equality, freedom, democracy, property, liberalism, the family and communitarianism. Recommended Preparation: Any Philosophy course. GNED Cluster 3 - Tier 3
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: PHIL
title: Theory of Knowledge
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture A study of some of the central problems in epistemology, including the following: knowledge and belief, empirical (perceptual and inductive) knowledge, a priori knowledge, appearance and reality, truth, and skepticism. Recommended Preparation: Any Philosophy course. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 3
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: PHIL
title: Philosophy of Science
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture For students in any discipline who would like to understand some of the fundamental principles of scientific enquiry and the place of scientific discoveries in human knowledge. Topics will include: scientific explanation, theory, prediction, confirmation, and, when feasible, other material relating to the interests of the students in the course. Recommended Preparation: Any Philosophy course. GNED Cluster 1 - Tier 3
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: PHIL
title: Philosophy of Mind
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture A study of topics such as: thought, emotions, action and the will, mind-body identity, personal identity, and theories about the nature of mind. Recommended Preparation: Any Philosophy course. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 3
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: PHIL
title: Philosophy and Pop Culture
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course brings philosophical reflection together with pop culture. It explores philosophical issues within pop culture (such as film, television, graphic novels, pop music, videos, games, social networks or advertising), as well as insights in philosophy about the nature of pop culture itself. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 2
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: PHIL
title: Philosophy and the Environment
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Traditional and contemporary philosophical theories and methods will be brought to bear on issues raised by the relationship between human beings and their environment. Moral and other philosophical aspects of some major issues, such as pollution, population growth, and use of natural resources, will be considered. Recommended Preparation: Any Philosophy course. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 3
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: PHIL
title: Directed readings
description: Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Directed Reading Subject to the approval of the Chair. Directed Readings are intended to provide a more flexible approach for students who want to pursue and receive credit in areas of study which are of particular interest to them. Two Directed Reading courses can be used for graduation purposes but they must be in different disciplines. A Directed Reading cannot replicate an existing course. The objectives of the Directed Reading course(s) must be filed in the Office of the Registrar and will be made available to any institution requesting them for evaluation purposes. Arrangements for Directed Readings must be completed on or before the Add/ Drop deadline.

subject: PHIL
title: Philosophy and Critical Health Studies
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course brings philosophy together with critical health studies, exploring a range of philosophical issues related to power, ethics, somatic practices, authority, and embodied experiences. Areas of philosophy will include philosophy of disability, as well as transdisciplinary areas like critical disability studies. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 3
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: PHIL
title: Language, Truth, and Adventures in Abstraction
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course explores topics in Anglo-American Philosophy, a blanket term covering questions and approaches which emerged as predominate in many English-speaking universities in the UK and North America in the 20th Century. Its hallmark is the thesis that philosophical reflection on language provides a methodological framework for addressing traditional philosophical problems. Its main branches include analytic philosophy, post-analytic philosophy, and philosophical pragmatism. The specific topics will vary from term to term, and may include such things as ‘ordinary language’ philosophy, logical positivism, theory of action and belief, the language of science and mathematics, logical analysis, and the relationship of truth and meaning.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: PHIL
title: Art, Beauty, Expression
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course explores topics in aesthetics or the philosophy of art. Topics will vary from term to term, but may include such things as the criteria of aesthetic evaluation, artistic representation vs. expression, meaning and meaningfulness, whether beauty is objective or subjective, art as a social phenomenon, and competing answers to the question “What is art?”.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: PHIL
title: Is, Ought, Why
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course explores topics in moral theory. In any given term, the course may focus on general theories or concepts, for example contemporary metaethics, utilitarianism, or theories of rights. Sometimes it may focus on a particular set of problems, such as moral personhood, freewill, or moral responsibility.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: PHIL
title: Power, Violence, Evil
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course explores topics in continental philosophy. Topics will vary from term to term, but may include themes such as desire and embodiment, anxiety and despair, freedom and choice, transcendence and immanence, social oppression and resistance, cultural and religious difference.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: PHIL
title: Philosophy of History
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course examines topics such as the relationship between fact and value in understanding history, the relevance of history to our understanding of human nature, the meaning of world history as a whole, theories of historical explanation and competing answers to the question “What is history?”
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: PHIL
title: Directed readings
description: Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Directed Reading Subject to the approval of the Chair. Directed Readings are intended to provide a more flexible approach for students who want to pursue and receive credit in areas of study which are of particular interest to them. Two Directed Reading courses can be used for graduation purposes but they must be in different disciplines. A Directed Reading cannot replicate an existing course. The objectives of the Directed Reading course(s) must be filed in the Office of the Registrar and will be made available to any institution requesting them for evaluation purposes. Arrangements for Directed Readings must be completed on or before the Add/ Drop deadline.

subject: PHIL
title: Advanced Philosophical Topics
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture While the study of philosophy involves engaging with canonical texts and thinkers, it extends to questions that expand the scope of research, illuminate problems, and compel methodological experiments. This seminar, designed for undergraduates with a background in philosophy, will enable students to bring disciplinary methods and debates to bear upon pressing contemporary issues in the discipline.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: PHYL
title: Teaching Games for Understanding
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This is an introductory course that utilizes theoretical underpinnings to promote teaching game strategy, tactics and skills in a logical, progressive manner. This course provides an overview of how game skills can contribute to healthy lifestyles and the development of age appropriate fundamental movement skills with a focus on activity for life.

subject: PHYL
title: Human Anatomy
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Lab Hour(s): 3 This course provides a regional approach to learning the structure of the human body. Topics will include: anatomical terminology, positions and movements; the anatomy of articulations, bones, muscles, and nerve tissue; and the regional anatomy of the head, thorax, abdomen, back, pelvis, upper limbs and lower limbs. Labs are designed to provide practical and interactive demonstrations that parallel the material discussed in the lectures. GNED Cluster 1 - Tier 2

subject: PHYL
title: Human Physiology
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Lab Hour(s): 3 This course outlines the basic principles and systems of human physiology including neural, muscular, sensory, sensory, cardiovascular, respiratory, renal and immune systems. Principles such as homeostasis, energy, and cellular metabolism will be applied to the understanding of physiological systems. Human health and performance will be considered when exploring these topics.
notes: Only one of BIOL 1216 or PHYL 2514 can be used to satisfy Bachelor of Health and Physical Education program requirements.

subject: PHYL
title: Introductory and Intermediate Coaching
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course introduces students to philosophy, making ethical decisions, teaching and learning, practice planning, growth and development, basic mental skills, designing a basic sport program and training methods as it pertains to coaching. Students will be introduced to the material included in the National Coaching Certification Program Part A and Part B of the Multi-Sport Competition Introduction Stream and the Long Term Athlete Development Model.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: PHYL
title: Foundations of Physical Literacy
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course is an introduction to physical literacy. This course provides foundational knowledge of the physical, cognitive, social and emotional domains of physical literacy. The construct of physical literacy will be explored across the sectors of sport, recreation and education.

subject: PHYL
title: Adapted Physical Activity
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course provides an examination of specific problems within the psychomotor domain and the related delivery systems for their identification and amelioration. This is an applied course that focuses on the issues and challenges of providing physical activity experiences for persons with a disability. GNED Cluster 3 - Tier 2

subject: PHYL
title: Psychology of Exercise
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture The aim of exercise psychology is to understand psychological influences behind exercise behaviour and to encourage healthy choices. In this course students will identify barriers to and opportunities for healthy behaviour from a psycho-social lens. Students will learn behaviour change theories to improve, reinforce and adhere to healthy behaviour.

subject: PHYL
title: Exercise Physiology
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Tutorial Hour(s): 2 This course will provide an overview of the human body’s response to acute and chronic exercise, exploring the principles of neuromuscular physiology, cardiorespiratory physiology, and energy metabolism. It will further examine physiological responses during exercise in altered temperature and atmospheric pressure.

subject: PHYL
title: Biomechanics
