description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture In this course, students move beyond the basics of public relations writing to explore writing for publication and specific genres, including employee communication, briefs, feature writing, report writing, pitches, and corporate profiles. Students will continue to hone their research, writing, editing and interviewing skills.

subject: PUBR
title: Media Relations
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course examines the relationship between public relations and the media. Through work on a client project, students learn about the importance of strategic media planning in both traditional and online environments and practice writing public service announcements (PSAs), news releases, feature articles and creating media kits. Students participate in a mock news conference and receive media training.

subject: PUBR
title: Internal Public Relations and Member Engagement
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course is designed to help students think critically about internal communication within the organizational context. Through theory-based lectures and experiential exercises, students will better understand the nature, strengths and limits of internal communication and its usefulness in fostering internal publics’ engagement, and how to apply theory learned in the classroom in the practice of public relations.

subject: PUBR
title: Public Relations Management
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Building on the Introduction to Public Relations course and Research Methods course, students learn the fundamentals of public relations program planning. The course begins with theory, case study analysis and discussion, and ends with teams planning, executing and evaluating a client-based public relations initiative. Students conduct research, analyze target audiences and messages to be delivered, undertake budgeting and scheduling activities, and evaluate the results.

subject: PUBR
title: Computer Mediated Communication
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course introduces public relations students to on-line/digital communication in both the classroom and the computer lab. Participants learn to use the technologies and understand them sufficiently to advise others on their applications, strengths and weaknesses. Students become familiar with digital uses for Internet and social applications as they relate to communications.

subject: PUBR
title: Research Methods for Public Relations
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course develops competence in major forms of research including the use of surveys, focus groups, content analysis, and the use of existing information accessed through libraries, online databases, and other computer-based search tools. Elements of descriptive statistics as they apply to the analysis of survey design are reviewed. The course provides instruction and hands-on practice in conducting communications research and communicating the findings as a fundamental part of the public relations profession.

subject: PUBR
title: Government Public Relations
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture In this course students will explore the critical role strategic public relations plays as organizations seek to inform and influence government policy-making processes and outcomes as well as examining the public relations function within government. Students will monitor current issues facing organizations; examine the theory, practice and context of government relations; and understand what it takes to create and manage an effective government relations program.

subject: PUBR
title: Strategic Social Media for Public Relations
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture In this course, students will examine the rich and complex picture of social media and the critical role strategic social media plays in the organizational public relations function. This course applies social media strategies to public relations for government, non-profit, corporate and agency sectors. It challenges students to understand the framework for transitioning social media from the personal to the organizational voice and to respond strategically to the way an organization’s audience connects, collaborates and builds relationships online. It focuses on strategic analysis and organizational integration of social media objectives through public relations strategies.

subject: PUBR
title: The Canadian Energy Industry, CSR and Sustainability PR
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course examines the critical and emerging trends associated with (1) the Energy Industry in Alberta and Canada, (2) Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR) and sustainability reporting, and (3) Environmentalism. The course provides a history of sustainability public relations practices focusing on the Canadian energy industries in relation to its associated stakeholders of environmental groups, First Nations communities, non-profits, think tanks and other affected political actors. Students’ learning is facilitated via contemporary case studies, guest speakers, academic literature, lectures and assignments.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: PUBR
title: Special Topics in Public Relations
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course provides an examination of selected topics designed to address critical or emerging trends in public relations. As this is a variable content course, the specific topic will be announced and advertised each semester it is offered. Students may not receive credit for this course more than once.

subject: PUBR
title: Strategic Communication Planning
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course is designed to build on material covered in the Public Relations Management and Work Experience I courses. Students will increase their understanding of strategic and operational planning concepts in the public relations context by offering consulting services to clients. Working in groups, students will review business plans, conduct a communications audit and write a comprehensive communication plan to assist their client organizations in achieving organizational goals.

subject: PUBR
title: Investor Relations
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This senior-level course for public relations students represents one of the key growth areas for the profession. Students examine the role of investor relations, its purpose within specific organizations, including the appropriate regulations, brokerage, security and ethical considerations of communicating in the financial marketplace. Financial communication documents are analyzed including proxies, disclosure documents, annual and quarterly reports, etc. Students learn how to successfully plan and manage an annual shareholder meeting, an investor presentation, and respond to the financial media.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: PUBR
title: International and Intercultural Public Relations
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This is a senior-level course for public relations students. Students learn the essential elements of effective public relations activities in an international and intercultural context. They examine those elements through the relevant policies and activities governing public relations abroad including marketing, advertising, cultural diversity issues, legislation and government regulations, as well as new communication technologies, financial markets, ethics, international labour, and workplace issues.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: PUBR
title: Fund Development and Corporate Philanthropy
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This is a senior-level course for public relations students, covering two aspects of social public relations - raising funds and creating programs that give financial resources away. This course covers such areas as: understanding government regulations, corporate giving guidelines, accessing funding agencies, understanding the options for fund maintenance, market and audience research, ethical considerations, and the public relations activities required to establish, maintain and promote partnerships with key stakeholders.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: PUBR
title: Issues and Ethics in Public Relations
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This is a senior course for students in their final semester of the public relations major in the Bachelor of Communication degree. The course, structured as a combination of senior seminar and directed reading, provides students the opportunity to reflect on the learning they have gained; examine the profession they are about to enter; contemplate and consider work experience placements and integrate these perspectives in a thesis of relevance to the profession and their future endeavours.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: PUBR
title: Issues Management, Risk and Crisis Communication
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture In this course, students will undertake a detailed examination of the principles and applications of the critical elements of sensitive issues management, risk and crisis communications within the context of the public relations responsibilities in organizational communication. The course will concentrate on theoretical considerations of such core topics as trust, effective spokespeople during crisis, credible messaging and stakeholder dialogue. It will address the fundamentals of issues management, effective implementation of crisis communication strategies and workable risk communication models.

subject: PUBR
title: Public Relations Workplace Issues and Preparation Seminar
description: Lecture Hour(s): 1.5 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This mandatory, zero-credit preparation seminar for work experience will examine conditions and issues which may arise in the workplace and the responsibilities and rights of employers and employees. Potential employers and working public relations professionals will be invited to present their perspectives and expectations as students develop resumes and portfolios and prepare to apply for work opportunities in a subsequent semester.
notes: The seminar must be completed as preparation for and in advance of, the required work experience. The seminar must be completed as preparation for and in advance of, the required work experience.

subject: PUBR
title: Public Relations Work Experience I
description: Other Hours Schedule Type: Work Term The work experiences required for the public relations major in communication studies offers students the opportunity to integrate the theory and practice of public relations learned in the classroom to supervised work experience in a public relations and communications management setting.

subject: PUBR
title: Public Relations Work Experience II
description: Other Hours Schedule Type: Work Term To further student industry experience, students can participate in an optional second work experience in the public relations major in communication studies. This work experience offers students the opportunity to integrate the theory and practice of public relations learned in the classroom to supervised work experience in a public relations and communications management setting.

subject: RELS
title: World Religions: Western
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course is an introduction to Western religions including Judaism, Christianity and Islam. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 2
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: RELS
title: World Religions: Eastern
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course is an introduction to Eastern religions such as Hinduism, Sikhism, Jainism, Buddhism, Confucianism, Daoism, and Shinto. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 2
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: RELS
title: Religion and Violence
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course introduces students to the study of religion by exploring the relationship between religion and violence. Themes covered may include: theories of religion and violence, self-harm, martyrdom, sacrifice, symbolic violence, resistance to religiously-sanctioned violence, religion and domestic violence, religion and non-violence. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 2
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: RELS
title: Why Religion Matters
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture An introduction to the study of religion that relates religious traditions to contemporary thought and culture. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 2
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: RELS
title: Religion and Popular Culture
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course examines religious beliefs, practices, institutions, etc as expressed in works of popular culture. The course may focus on religion in a specific type of media (e.g. film, TV, literature, art) or on a religious theme (e.g. apocalypticism or the environment). GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 2
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: RELS
title: Religious Experience
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course examines a variety of types of religious experience (e.g. communal solidarity, moral development, conversion, mysticism, possession, trance, ecstasy). Emphasis will be placed on different theoretical accounts of the origin, nature and/or significance of these experiences. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 3

subject: RELS
title: Religion and Ecology
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course examines how religious traditions may hinder or advance progress on environmental protection and sustainability. This course will introduce students to the work of leading scholars in this field enabling us to explore how religions influence views about the relationship between humans and nature, humans and non-human animals, and questions of stewardship or dominance. Students will also consider what religious views and practices are consonant with living sustainably. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 2
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: RELS
title: Chinese Religions
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course will introduce the major religious traditions of China, including Confucianism, Daoism, Buddhism, and popular Chinese religion. Examining the history, teachings, and practices of these traditions will provide insight into Chinese religion and culture. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 3
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: RELS
title: Good and Evil
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course examines religious views of moral issues with an emphasis on the nature and consequences of right action. The course may focus on dualistic worldviews (i.e., ethics as a struggle between good and evil) and/or on karmic and nondualistic worldviews. Issues may include interpersonal relations, social justice, treatment of non-humans, sexuality, violence, and biomedical advances. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 3
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: RELS
title: Sikhism
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course is an introduction to Sikh religion and identity. Particular attention will be given to the historical context from which Sikhism arose, the worldviews and goals it articulates, the development and content of its authoritative literature, and its religious practices. Sikhism outside of the Indian context may also be discussed. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 3
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: RELS
title: Hinduism
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course is an examination of the major religious developments in Hinduism from ancient times to the modern period. The course introduces students to the central thinkers, literature, beliefs, and practices associated with Hinduism. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 2
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: RELS
title: Christianity
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course is an overview of Christianity, including Orthodoxy, Catholicism and Protestantism. Topics may include beliefs, rituals, institutions, experts, art, architecture, artifacts and popular religiosity. The focus is contemporary, with some historical background. Christianity in Canada and relations between Christianity and society will be addressed: for example, issues of politics, economics, ethics, mass media, gender, race and/or class. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 2
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: RELS
title: Islam
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course is an introduction to Islam from its origins in the Arabian peninsula to contemporary times as a global religion. Attention will be given to the historical and cultural contexts in which Islam arose, to the worldviews and goals it articulates, to the development and content of its literature, and to its religious practices. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 2
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: RELS
title: Judaism
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course provides an introduction to the history, practices, beliefs, institutions and literature of the Jewish religion, from biblical times until the modern era. The course will describe the distinctive features associated with Judaism in different time periods, and the values, beliefs, and rituals that developed in response to the historical and cultural settings encountering Judaism. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 2
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: RELS
title: Buddhism
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture A study of the doctrinal development and practices of the Buddhist tradition, including treatment of topics such as early Buddhism, Mahayana, and Tibetan Vajrayana. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 3

subject: RELS
title: Gender, Sexuality and Religion
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course examines the origins, content, and influence of the views of sex, gender, and sexuality in various religious traditions and practices. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 3
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: RELS
title: Directed Readings
description: Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Directed Reading Subject to the approval of the Chair. Directed Readings are intended to provide a more flexible approach for students who want to pursue and receive credit in areas of study which are of particular interest to them. Two Directed Reading courses can be used for graduation purposes but they must be in different disciplines. A Directed Reading cannot replicate an existing course. The objectives of the Directed Reading course(s) must be filed in the Office of the Registrar and will be made available to any institution requesting them for evaluation purposes. Arrangements for Directed Readings must be completed on or before the Add/ Drop deadline.

subject: RELS
title: Selected Topics in Religion
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course provides an in-depth examination of a selected topic or theme in the study of religion. Possible topics include religion and the body; religion and the environment; religious fundamentalism; religion and sexuality. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 3
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: RELS
title: Esotericism, Magic and the Occult
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course offers an historical and thematic introduction to Western Esotericism. Topics may include Gnosticism, Hermeticism, Astrology, Alchemy, Kabbalah, Naturphilosophie, Rosicrucianism, Freemasonry, Mesmerism, Spiritualism, Theosophy, Anthroposophy, the modern occult (e.g., Eliphas Levi and Aleister Crowley), Traditionalism, New Age and Neopaganism. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 3

subject: RELS
title: Religion in Contemporary East Asia
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course will examine the ways that religious traditions have adapted to modern conditions in East Asian countries such as China, Japan, and Korea. Possible topics include: Buddhist monasticism, socially-engaged Buddhism; pilgrimage and tourism, married monks, and ritual interactions with ghosts. Recommended Preparation: One of RELS 2212 or RELS 2279 . GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 3
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: RELS
title: Religion in the Americas
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course examines religion in the Americas today (especially Canada, the US, Mexico and Brazil) along with relevant historical developments. Topics may include the impacts of colonization and immigration, indigenous traditions, Church-State relations, folk Catholicism, liberation theology, Afro-Christian traditions and Spiritism, and (neo-)Pentecostalisms. GNED Cluster 3 - Tier 3
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: RELS
title: Death and the Afterlife
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course examines various religious perspectives on suffering, death, and the afterlife. The death rituals of different religions may also be considered. GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 3
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: RELS
title: Topics in Hinduism
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course is an in-depth study of a specific Hindu thinker, text, movement, or issue. Course content will vary from year to year. Possible topics include the Bhagavad Gita, Gandhi, the poet saints, Shankara, and the Upanishads. Recommended Preparation: RELS 2252 . GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 3

subject: RELS
title: Topics in Sikhism
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course is an in-depth study of a specific Sikh thinker, text, movement, or issue. Course content will vary from year to year. Possible topics include the Dasam Granth, Guru Arjan, the Namdhari tradition, the rahit tradition, and the Singh Sabha movement. Recommended Preparation: RELS 2251 . GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 3

subject: RELS
title: Topics in Christianity
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course is an in depth study of a specific Christian thinker, text, movement, or issue. Course content will vary from year to year. Possible topics include globalized Christianity, Christian mysticism, and Christianity and the World Wide Web. Recommended Preparation: RELS 2253 . GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 3

subject: RELS
title: Yoga and Meditation
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course examines the history, theory, and practices of yoga and meditation from their Asian origins to their global spread in modern times. Topics examined may include Buddhist and Daoist traditions of meditation, the origins of yoga in India, Tantric yoga and meditation, and yoga and meditation in North America. Recommended Preparation: Any of RELS 1103 , RELS 2279 , or RELS 2252 . GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 3
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: RELS
title: Directed Readings
description: Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Directed Reading Subject to the approval of the Chair. Directed Readings are intended to provide a more flexible approach for students who want to pursue and receive credit in areas of study which are of particular interest to them. Two Directed Reading courses can be used for graduation purposes but they must be in different disciplines. A Directed Reading cannot replicate an existing course. The objectives of the Directed Reading course(s) must be filed in the Office of the Registrar and will be made available to any institution requesting them for evaluation purposes. Arrangements for Directed Readings must be completed on or before the Add/ Drop deadline.

subject: RELS
title: Religion and Public Life
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course explores the role of religion in public life. It examines how religion manifests in public spaces and institutions, and considers the challenges and possibilities of religious diversity in contemporary society. Topics to be examined may include religion and politics, healthcare, poverty, education, food, art, human rights, law, public policy, ethnicity, immigration, and multiculturalism. Recommended Preparation: One of RELS 2208 , RELS 2209 , RELS 2212 , RELS 2243 , RELS 2251 , RELS 2252 , RELS 2254 , RELS 2255 , RELS 2279 , RELS 2281 , RELS 3199 , RELS 3302 , RELS 3305 , RELS 3312 , RELS 3322 , RELS 3333 , RELS 3352 , RELS 3353 , RELS 3360 , RELS 3378 , RELS 4199 or RELS 4403 GNED Cluster 3 - Tier 3
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: RELS
title: Asian Religions in North America
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course explores Hindu, Sikh, Buddhist, and/or Chinese religious communities in North America. Attention will be given to one or more of these communities in Calgary. Recommended Preparation: One of RELS 2251 , RELS 2252 , RELS 2279 , RELS 3312 , RELS 3352 , or RELS 3353 . GNED Cluster 2 - Tier 3
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: SCIE
title: Introduction to Science
description: Lecture Hour(s): 4 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This is a credit-free upgrading course; special fees apply. Topics include zoology, botany, microbiology, biochemistry, chemistry, Newtonian and post-Newtonian physics.
notes: This course is the equivalent of Science 10. Not intended for science majors. This course is the equivalent of Science 10. Not intended for science majors.

subject: SCIE
title: Science of the Environment
description: Lecture Hour(s): 4 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Lab Hour(s): 2 This is a credit-free upgrading course; special fees apply. This course explores physics, chemistry, biology and environmental science. Topics include: electric and magnetic field theory and the EM spectrum; acids, bases, organic compounds, pollutants and their effect on the environment; how the human body interacts with the environment including the circulatory and immune systems; the impact of the environment on genetics; conventional and alternative sources of energy and renewable resources. Corresponding Alberta High School Equivalent: Science 30. Recommended Preparation: Science 20 or Physics 20 AND Chemistry 20 or Physics 20 AND CHEM 0115 .

subject: SCIE
title: Understanding and Translating Scientific Research
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course focuses on developing essential skills in analyzing, interpreting, integrating, and communicating scientific information. Students will learn to critically evaluate current scientific literature from across different disciplines, identify and contextualize key findings, and communicate complex ideas clearly and effectively in various formats, including academic publications, presentations, and public outreach. As a preparatory course for the senior capstone course, this course equips students with practical tools to bridge the gap between scientific research and broader audiences.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: SCIE
title: Science and Indigenous Knowledge
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course explores the integration of Indigenous knowledge in science with a focus on scientific studies that incorporate First Nations, Metis and Inuit knowledge. Students will examine the intersection between traditional wisdom and current scientific paradigms and evaluate the contributions of Indigenous knowledge to various disciplines of science.

subject: SCIE
title: General Science Senior Student Seminar
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This capstone course will help to prepare students for further academic studies and research using a student-centred learning approach. Course learning outcomes include a critique of scientific research, critical examination of controversial scientific ethical issues, presentation of work to peers, and peer and self-evaluation.
notes: This course should be taken immediately preceding SCIE 5020 . General Science Option
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: SCIE
title: General Science Interdisciplinary Project
description: Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Independent Study This capstone course will help students to use their academic learning to investigate a real-world problem within an interdisciplinary student group. Projects will be student-driven, but progress will be facilitated and assessed by one or more instructors with relevant expertise.
notes: This course should be taken immediately after SCIE 5010 . General Science Option

subject: SCIE
title: Work Integrated Learning in General Science
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Other Hour(s): 6 Other Hours Schedule Type: Internship Participate in a work-integrated learning experience applying theoretical knowledge and practical skills in a workplace environment of a local organization. Investigate a science-related issue identified by the organization and produce a deliverable for this project. The specific deliverable varies based on the nature of the placement, the academic background of the student, and the needs of the organization. Specific projects could involve collection, sorting, or analysis of information; summarizing or interpreting information; or using problem-solving skills to interrogate an issue.
notes: The course will run three hours of lecture for the first three weeks, with the rest of the semester being internship hours.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: SINV
title: Changing the World: An Introduction to Social Innovation
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Are you interested in creating positive social, economic, or environmental impact? Do you think you might be a budding changemaker, or possibly a disruptive social innovator? Social innovation explores ways to create lasting social change and impact. Learn about the intricacies of fair trade and the circular economy, the power of social movements, the emergence of social enterprise and B Corps, and the growing list of social innovations/innovators impacting our world today, culminating in an end-of-term Social Innovation Lab! GNED Cluster 3 - Tier 2

subject: SINV
title: Social Innovation Tools for Changemakers
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Become a social innovation thinker and work to design a more resilient, sustainable future! This course will focus on ideas about social innovation (thinking, doing and being) using case studies and thinking forward. Students will explore how change happens, and develop applicable tools and projects for their own social innovations.

subject: SINV
title: Social Innovation: Directed Readings
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Directed Reading Subject to the approval of the Chair. Directed Readings are intended to provide a more flexible approach for students who want to pursue and receive credit in areas of study which are of particular interest to them. Two Directed Reading courses can be used for graduation purposes but they must be in different disciplines. A Directed Reading cannot replicate an existing course. The objectives of the Directed Reading course(s) must be filed in the Office of the Registrar and will be made available to any institution requesting them for evaluation purposes. Arrangements for Directed Readings must be completed on or before the Add/ Drop deadline.

subject: SINV
title: The Facilitator’s Toolbox: Inspiring Innovation, Creativity, & Change
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Are you eager to become a versatile and skilled facilitator, capable of fostering innovation, nurturing creativity, and driving transformative change? In this dynamic course, you will unlock the secrets of “The Facilitator’s Toolbox.” Your learning journey will equip you with a comprehensive set of tools, techniques, and strategies that will help you inspire breakthroughs, foster creativity, and catalyze positive change. By nurturing collaboration and open communication, you will learn how to guide teams to unleash their full potential.

subject: SINV
title: Systems Thinking
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course considers how storytelling helps us grasp the nature of a complex system, how storytelling helps us define a problem within such a system, and how leaders can use storytelling to help their organization/initiative navigate a complex system. This course will introduce protocols surrounding a wide range of storytelling traditions as actionable concepts in addressing design problems.

subject: SINV
title: Agents of Social Change
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Successful social Innovators mix experimentation and reflection to make durable change; this class will allow you to develop your social innovator and systems leadership skills through thinking and action. As a CSL-designated class, we work with a wide variety of social innovators and social entrepreneurs across Calgary and Alberta. Students will create meaningful projects, such as program evaluations, learning tool kits, social enterprise prototypes, and community engagement plans, to name a few. Our CSL partners will also help you make the direct connection between class concepts and the very real social change work happening across our province. GNED Cluster 3 - Tier 3, Community Service Learning
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: SINV
title: Social Innovation: Directed Readings
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Directed Reading Subject to the approval of the Chair. Directed Readings are intended to provide a more flexible approach for students who want to pursue and receive credit in areas of study which are of particular interest to them. Two Directed Reading courses can be used for graduation purposes but they must be in different disciplines. A Directed Reading cannot replicate an existing course. The objectives of the Directed Reading course(s) must be filed in the Office of the Registrar and will be made available to any institution requesting them for evaluation purposes. Arrangements for Directed Readings must be completed on or before the Add/ Drop deadline.

subject: SINV
title: Special Topics in Social Innovation
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course provides an examination of selected topics in social innovation and those relevant to the values-driven business, the public sector and the social economy - community, volunteer and nonprofit organizations. As this is a variable content course, the specific topic will be announced and advertised each time it is offered. Students may not receive credit for this course more than once. GNED Cluster 3 - Tier 3
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: SINV
title: Civic Innovation - Igniting Change for Social Impact
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Welcome to the world of civic innovation, where imagination meets action in pursuit of positive societal transformation! In this inspiring course, you will embark on a journey that explores the art of driving change for social impact through innovative civic solutions. Civic Innovation goes beyond traditional approaches, by embracing creativity, empathy, and collaboration to address complex social challenges. Together, we will engage in thought-provoking discussions, hands-on exercises, and real-world case studies as we explore our community together.

subject: SINV
title: Design Thinking
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Design thinking is where innovation meets empathy. In this experiential course you will embark on a transformative journey to master the art of design thinking through human-centred design principles. These are structured approaches that use the tools of story, strategic foresight, and intentional participatory design to imagine and, in some cases, implement significant and just systemic transformations.

subject: SINV
title: Social Entrepreneurship - Disrupting the Status Quo
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Social entrepreneurship is a dynamic and innovative course that explores the principles and practices of creating sustainable and impactful solutions to society’s most pressing problems. This course examines the link between innovation and entrepreneurship using a ‘social’ lens by studying the ‘why’ of social entrepreneurship along with the ‘how’. A global movement, social entrepreneurship has captured the imagination of people worldwide who are part of a seismic shift that is disrupting the status quo.

subject: SINV
title: Social Innovation Strategy and Action
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This capstone course introduces the fundamentals of strategic planning and transformative values-centred management for projects in the business sector, the public sector, organizations participating in the social economy, Indigenous economies, or social innovation start-ups. A variety of perspectives, models and approaches are presented. Factors such as organizational leadership, culture, complexity and dynamic issues external to the organization are covered.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: SLGY
title: Introduction to Sociology
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Sociology is the investigation of society, ranging from everyday lived experiences to social institutions and organizations. With a commitment to social justice, a variety of topics will be explored, including social theories, social research methods, and social structures, with a strong focus on gender, sexualities, class, and racial inequality. The content will include topics and sociologists historically excluded from sociology. GNED Cluster 3 - Tier 2

subject: SLGY
title: Classical Sociological Theory
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course offers students an overview of the development of sociological theory with emphasis on the period from the nineteenth century to the Second World War. Theorists such as Marx, Durkheim and Weber will be reviewed and analyzed.

subject: SLGY
title: Sociology of Black and African Canadians
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture The course will explore the political, economic, and socio-cultural life of Black and African Canadians from a sociological perspective. Topics that may be covered in this class include the historical and ongoing connections to Africa and the African diaspora, colonialism, immigration, racism, sports, education, and employment.

subject: SLGY
title: Deviance and Society
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Deviance is an area within sociology referring to any behaviour, belief system, physical characteristic, symbolic representation, or group affiliation which may mark people as wrong, immoral, criminal, mentally unstable, subhuman, or otherwise. In general, deviance goes against cultural norms and expectations. This course explores such phenomena and students will learn to approach them theoretically.

subject: SLGY
title: Crime and Society
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course serves as an introduction to the sociological study of crime. The goal is to critically assess crime as it intersects with race, class, and gender. Students will become familiar with classical and contemporary theories of crime as well as strategies toward rehabilitation or social change.

subject: SLGY
title: Social Research Methods
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course introduces students to the fundamentals and techniques of social research methods. Students will learn about the research process in both quantitative and qualitative methods, including research orientations, design, ethics, sampling, data collection, analysis, and interpretation involved in conducting sociological research.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: SLGY
title: Sociology of Gender
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Gender operates on four dimensions in Canadian society: within individuals; through interaction with others; in the features of social institutions such as work, law, and family; and in the norms and values that shape the socio-cultural landscape. This course will introduce students to gender on each of these dimensions.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: SLGY
title: Sociology of Health and Illness
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course examines health, illness, and health care systems from a critical and structural perspective. Students will examine how various understandings of health and illness, and the institutions that monitor them, have emerged and changed geographically and historically. In addition, issues such as viability of the Canadian public health care system and privatization will be discussed.

subject: SLGY
title: The Sociology of Indigenous Peoples
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course will address the topics of history, colonization, decolonization, and society through an Indigenous sociological lens. The course will focus on dominant areas of sociological study and analysis, including inequalities of race, gender, health, religion, education, justice, the economy, culture, and social class. Instead of following traditional sociological paradigms, emphasis will be on both how colonization and capitalism has shaped these areas of social life, and how Indigenous peoples in Canada and abroad are continuously advancing theories and practices to deal with these social structures and processes.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: SLGY
title: Urban Sociology
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture The course introduces students to the field of urban sociology. Through a focus on social interaction, students will investigate how power and inequality are produced, reproduced and also destabilized in urban social spaces. Specific topics that will be covered include theories of the city, urban ethnography, urban subcultures, forms of urban social resistance and urban design and control.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: SLGY
title: Environmental Sociology
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture In this introduction to the sociological study of environmental issues, students will learn about the relationship between human society and the environment from a critical, anti-racist, eco-feminist perspective, with a focus on environmental justice and environmental movements. Students will also consider the relationship and tensions between capitalism and the environment.

subject: SLGY
title: Social Class, Inequality and Poverty
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Social inequality is one of the major problems of our time. This course will explore the structural nature of class, inequality, and poverty in Canada and globally. Undertaking this will inevitably direct our attention to issues of fairness and social justice.

subject: SLGY
title: Sociology of Families
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course offers a sociological investigation into families. The focus will be on theories of family and the social history of families. Some specific topics covered include the structure and function of families, family systems, family life stages (dating, marriage, parenting and old age), diverse family forms, and problems in family life such as violence and divorce.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: SLGY
title: Sociology of Aging
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Individuals experience changing roles, activities, and relationships as they age. In addition, population aging, caused by declining birthrates and increasing life expectancies, has profound implications for social structures, norms, and values. This course examines the social aspects of both individual and population aging.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: SLGY
title: Theories of “Race” and Ethnicity
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course engages in a critical historical/theoretical investigation of the race concept. The focus is on race and ethnicity as experienced in everyday life. There is strong emphasis on reading and evaluating scholarly work on race/ethnicity and, in particular, on racism. While race and ethnic oppression are stressed, forms of resistance to racism and collective solidarity based on anti-racist organizing are also explored.

subject: SLGY
title: Sociology of Religion
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course offers an introduction to sociological concepts and theories in the study of religion. Questions about the structure of religious organizations, creation of meaning, secularization and religious diversity will be addressed.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: SLGY
title: Selected Topics: Social Problems
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture The specific topics of this course will vary based on the expertise of the faculty members in sociology.

subject: SLGY
title: Practicing Sociology: Skills for Career and Academic Success - Work-Integrated Learning
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Other Hour(s): 1 Other Hours Schedule Type: Experiential Learning This course introduces key skills for career and academic readiness by emphasizing the practical application of sociological theoretical and research concepts to real-world issues. Each student will be paired with a community partner to complete a micro-internship. This course bridges academic learning with hands-on application, providing students with insights into how sociological knowledge can assist with their career objectives and educational goals.

subject: SLGY
title: Directed Readings
description: Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Directed Reading Subject to the approval of the Chair. Directed Readings are intended to provide a more flexible approach for students who want to pursue and receive credit in areas of study which are of particular interest to them. Two Directed Reading courses can be used for graduation purposes but they must be in different disciplines. A Directed Reading cannot replicate an existing course. The objectives of the Directed Reading course(s) must be filed in the Office of the Registrar and will be made available to any institution requesting them for evaluation purposes. Arrangements for Directed Readings must be completed on or before the Add/ Drop deadline.

subject: SLGY
title: Migration, Borders and (Im)mobilities
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course will examine the contemporary migration and im(mobility) of people across borders. Central to this course will be an understanding of how colonialism, racism, and sexism intersect with migration, identity politics, state policies, and international governance of migration.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: SLGY
title: Sociology of Sexualities
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course examines sexualities through a critical sociological lens. The goal is to track how and why the predominant conceptions of sexuality have been constructed, with particular attention to the role of binary sexualities and subsequent concepts of ‘deviation,’ as well as how sexuality is used in and by mechanisms/institutions of power

subject: SLGY
title: Social Movements
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course introduces students to the field of social movements. It examines how contemporary social movements have in some ways continued and in other ways transformed our understanding of sociological concepts and issues such as gender and racial inequality/diversity/solidarity, modes of protest and social change, and the politics of culture.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: SLGY
title: The Sociology of the Body
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course focuses on matters of the body which affect social life. Through an examination of theoretical articles upon such topics as race, gender, disease, sexuality, smell, and appearance, we will learn about the many ways perceptions of bodies affect our lives and how society in turn defines and influences appearance and behaviour.

subject: SLGY
title: Quantitative Methods
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course focuses on the development of quantitative analytical skills in sociology. Students will answer sociological questions through computerized analysis of survey data. They will also be required to interpret the output from quantitative analyses and to discuss how their findings inform relevant sociological debates. Specific procedures covered include t-tests, correlation, and regression methods.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: SLGY
title: Qualitative Research Methods
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course presents an overview of qualitative methods in sociological research. Students will examine the strengths and weaknesses of qualitative methods such as interviewing techniques, fieldwork, and the case study approach, as well as how their lives - as researchers - intersect with and influence the people and situations they study.

subject: SLGY
title: Power, Politics, and Resistance
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course examines power as a central framework for understanding political life in a global context. It analyzes how colonialism, capitalism, and gender shape the formation of states as institutions of coercion, regulation, and redistribution that classify and stratify subjects. Students will explore how people respond to and contest state actions, and consider the emergence, transformation, and uncertain futures of state power and political order.

subject: SLGY
title: Contemporary Social Theory
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course explores several major currents of contemporary social thought since the World War Two era. Some of the theoretical perspectives that will be investigated are Western Marxism, Post-Structuralism, Post-Modernism, and theories of Globalisation/Post-Colonialism. These will be explored through their applicability to sociological issues of interest, such as subjectivity, agency/structure, power, and modernity.

subject: SLGY
title: Gender and Work
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Gender has a profound impact on the opportunities and experiences of individuals in both paid and unpaid work. This course will analyze the socially constructed, gendered nature of men’s and women’s work.

subject: SLGY
title: Sociology of Science
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This is a course on the production and dissemination of scientific knowledge. Specifically, it focuses on how the social, political, economic and cultural contexts in which scientists work affect the type of knowledge that they produce. Topics covered in this course may include various theories of scientific knowledge, the process of knowledge creation and dissemination, the production of certainty and uncertainty, the funding context for science, how social inequalities affect the careers of scientists, the privatization of scientific research and dissemination, scientific innovation, and newer community-based models for science.

subject: SLGY
title: Practicing Public Sociology - Work Integrated Learning
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Other Hour(s): 2 Other Hours Schedule Type: Experiential Learning This course offers a critical study of public and applied sociology with a focus on how to apply sociological skills and knowledge beyond the university. Central to this course will be an opportunity to apply sociological skills in a work-integrated learning context such as with government departments or agencies, not-for-profit organizations, Indigenous governments and organizations, or private sector companies. Possible topics may include the application of sociology in migration, globalization, Indigenous rights, human rights, health, disability, environment, data and policy analysis, social justice, or social services.

subject: SLGY
title: Mass Communication
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course engages in an analysis of the pervasive presence of mass media in modern society and the many ways that various forms of media representation intersect with mass audiences, An overview of the development of modern media is extended to include a summary of the key perspectives on media texts and how they position the audience.

subject: SLGY
title: Methodologies for Indigenous Sociologies
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course focuses on Indigenous sociological epistemologies and methodologies. Colonial societies will be deconstructed through an Indigenous lens that challenges traditional sociological methodologies. Indigenous peoples’ experiences in these societies, such as in Abya-Yala (Latin America) and Turtle Island (North America), will provide students a comprehensive understanding of how Indigenous sociological methodologies are applied to investigate, analyze, explain, and solve social issues such as food and water supply, governance, environment and society, social inequality, urbanization, and social justice.

subject: SLGY
title: Sociology of Economic Life
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Using a sociological lens to examine markets, networks, social stratification, economic development and globalization reveals ways in which these diverse economic phenomena are embedded in societies. In addition, the sociological approach reveals how consumption is connected to status symbols, how gender affects work and pay, and how international production and trade can challenge national sovereignty.

subject: SLGY
title: The Socialized Self
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Drawing on a number of interpretive sociological perspectives, this course explores the process of socialization and the related process of the development of the self. Key concepts in the study of socialization such as role, identity, institution and ideology will be critically investigated, as will the role of socialization processes in the perpetuation of inequality and domination.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: SLGY
title: Sociology of Violence
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course is designed to encourage students to question and examine how our ability and inability to understand violence shapes the manner in which it is analyzed, and both legitimated and de-legitimated on academic, social, political and cultural levels. Violence will be examined sociologically as it relates to social relations, religion, gender, identity, and culture.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: SLGY
title: Selected Topics: Social Issues
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This advanced seminar introduces students to pressing and timely social issues and problems. It may focus on a variety of themes, for example: environmental issues, white collar crime, urban sustainability, war and genocide, or social movements.

subject: SLGY
title: Intersections in Social Life
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This dialogue-based course gives students an opportunity to assess the interlocking nature of the forces of social domination and resistance such as those based on social class, nationalism, race, gender, sexuality, religion, and politics. The class will investigate together these intersections in social life through critical reading and writing and through engagement in the process of individual and collective reflection on both our inner and outer worlds and experiences.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: SLGY
title: Directed Readings
description: Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Directed Reading Subject to the approval of the Chair. Directed Readings are intended to provide a more flexible approach for students who want to pursue and receive credit in areas of study which are of particular interest to them. Two Directed Reading courses can be used for graduation purposes but they must be in different disciplines. A Directed Reading cannot replicate an existing course. The objectives of the Directed Reading course(s) must be filed in the Office of the Registrar and will be made available to any institution requesting them for evaluation purposes. Arrangements for Directed Readings must be completed on or before the Add/ Drop deadline.

subject: SLGY
title: Sociology of Food
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course introduces students to the significance of the relationships between food, people and society. Even though eating is an individual choice it is affected by society at large. Thus we will explore the social, economic, political, and symbolic dimensions of food systems and food behavior to gain a better understanding of the social determinants of food and eating. The primary aim of this course is to help students understand social inequality through the lens of food.

subject: SLGY
title: Sociology of Disaster
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course is an introduction to the sociological study of disasters and their effect on human populations. The goal of the course is to understand how floods, hurricanes, heat waves, oil spills and other catastrophic events provide a “strategic research site” where we can refine our ideas and assumptions about social life and inequality.

subject: SLGY
title: (De)Constructing Health and Medicine
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course examines the changing conceptions of medicine and medical knowledge. More specially, it looks at how health, illness, and disease are defined and the implications of such representations and understandings. Particular attention is given to the ways in which medical knowledge invokes, represents, and implicates gender, race, class and sexuality.

subject: SLGY
title: Mind and Society
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Drawing on existential sociology and related perspectives (e.g., phenomenology and symbolic interactionism) and insights from the Sociology of Knowledge, this course critically examines one of the most elusive of sociological phenomena, the “mind” and how this social construct is intimately linked to the patterns we perceive as “society”.

subject: SLGY
title: Economic Development and Social Change
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course examines the processes of global economic development and social change through a comparative historical lens. It pays particular attention to how the conceptualization of development has varied according to changing societal conditions. The course also explores the controversies within sociology on how best to achieve progressive national development.

subject: SLGY
title: Structure and Subjectivities
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Located in the debates between micro and macro theories of society, this course explores the connections between structure and agency through the ways in which the self is constructed. The goal is to understand how our sense of self is constituted to allow governance, but also to allow agency in the form of resistance.

subject: SLGY
title: Feminist Theories
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This advanced theory course examines a range of feminist theories. The contents of this course will speak back to and disrupt the sociological canons. Students of this course will engage with theoretical works traditionally excluded from the realm of sociological theories.

subject: SLGY
title: Selected Topics: Inequality
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This advanced seminar examines the creation and maintenance of inequality, as well as actions for ameliorating its negative effects. It may focus upon a variety of themes, such as, global gender inequality, demographic inequality, and fair-trade initiatives. Students will consider both the degree of inequality and its existence as an unintended consequence of social arrangements.

subject: SLGY
title: Selected Topics: Social Research
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course examines relevant approaches to sociological research. Having completed the required series of research methods courses in sociology, this course allows students to advance their research skills with a focused application of methodological techniques. Students may learn and apply advanced techniques from quantitative, qualitative, or emerging methods relevant to sociology. The course culminates in a project involving the in-depth analysis, interpretation, and communication of sociological research.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: SLGY
title: Selected Topics: Social Theory
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This advanced course examines relevant currents in social theory. It deeply delves into theoretical ideas that underpin and shape sociological understandings of the social world. Students will critically examine the construction of theory, explore its limitations, and analyze the ways in which theory comes to form the basis of the sociological imagination.

subject: SLGY
title: Intersections in Social Life
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This seminar-based course gives students an opportunity to assess the interlocking nature of the forces of social domination such as class, race, gender, sexuality, religion, and politics. Students will investigate such intersections in social life through critical reading and writing and through engagement with the process of individual and collective reflection.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: SLGY
title: Honours Research I
description: Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Independent Study Students of Sociology 5110, working under the one-on-one supervision of a full-time faculty member, will develop and publicly present a research proposal to engage in sociological inquiry. The proposed project plan will be carried out in the second required course for the Sociology Honours stream, SLGY 5120 .
notes: Restricted to students officially admitted and registered in the Sociology Honours Program.

subject: SLGY
title: Honours Research II
description: Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Independent Study Students of Sociology 5120, working under the one-on-one supervision of a full-time faculty member, will execute the proposed project plan developed in SLGY 5110 (Honours Research I). In this research project, the student, in consultation with a supervisor, will engage in original sociological inquiry culminating in an honours thesis and presentation.
notes: Restricted to students officially admitted and registered in the Sociology Honours Program.

subject: SLWK
title: Introduction to Social Work History, Philosophy and Theory
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course provides an introduction to the historical, philosophical, ethical, and theoretical perspectives of social work practice. Factors distinguishing social work practice will be introduced, including social work values, ethics, theories, and models of practice. This course also examines the impact of oppression and marginalization on individuals, children and families, and communities, including the role of social work in contributing to colonization and decolonization in Canada.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: SLWK
title: Social Welfare Policies and Issues
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course is designed to help students become critically aware of the economic, social, and political environment within which they will eventually practice. It will examine the process by which social policy is developed in Canada and encourage reflection upon the ways social workers are influenced by, and in turn can influence, that process. A review and analysis of social problems, policies, and issues as they relate to the delivery and development of appropriate social services will occur. Students will be invited to examine their own values, as well as dominant ideologies present within Canada today.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: SLWK
title: Social Work Practice I
description: Lecture Hour(s): 2 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Seminar Other Hour(s): 6.5 Other Hours Schedule Type: Field Work Social Work Practice I provides students with an opportunity to observe and engage in direct practice with individuals, groups, or communities. This introductory practicum will expose students to the complexity of practice issues related to work in human services and to view societal issues from a social work perspective. During this first practicum experience, students are challenged to examine how their social location influences their work with clients or client groups. Students are expected to demonstrate beginner interviewing and assessment skills and an introductory understanding of social work as a distinct profession.

subject: SLWK
title: Social Work Assessment and Documentation
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course provides students with an opportunity to develop the knowledge and skills required to gather and evaluate information using a variety of evidence-informed approaches and tools. Integrating an intersectional approach to trauma, family violence and mental health, students will explore topics including assessment, case planning, goal setting, intervention, evaluation and documentation.

subject: SLWK
title: Counselling Skills
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This experiential course concentrates on the development of interviewing and counselling skills with an emphasis on social work practice with individuals, children and families. Students will have the opportunity to develop an introductory therapeutic skill set through simulated counselling interviews.

subject: SLWK
title: Interprofessional Practice and Leadership in Social Work Organizations
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Social work organizations require strong and effective leadership. This course will provide students with an understanding of how to lead social service agencies and programs in order to provide a healthy working environment that promotes effective service provision focused on client empowerment. Students will explore topics related to organizational development, program planning, and leadership in nonprofit organizations.

subject: SLWK
title: Social Work Knowledge and Skills for Group Work
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course introduces and provides an overview of social work practice with groups through a combination of lectures, observation, and experiential learning. The stages of group development, group roles and norms, facilitation, group design, implementation and evaluation will be addressed. Students will have the opportunity to develop their facilitation skills through participation in group simulations.

subject: SLWK
title: Social Work Practice with Communities
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course provides students with an introduction to community social work practice. The course will address the role of community social work practice in the context of diversity, social change movements, and activism. Students will explore a range of theoretical frameworks for community change and develop skills to work in partnership with diverse communities to create change.

subject: SLWK
title: Social Work Practice with Families
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course will focus on social work practice with family systems. Students will develop an understanding of the interactive, ecological, socioeconomic, and cultural influences on families. Attention will be paid to the impacts of current and historical trauma, engaging families and assessing for strengths, sources of resilience and support, and pathways to change. Students will be introduced to strategies and methods to enhance resilience, safety and connection to community and culture utilizing both formal and natural supports.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: SLWK
title: Social Work Practice in Addictions and Mental Health
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course provides students with an understanding of the intersectional factors that affect mental health. Students will explore the interactions between mental health, trauma and substance abuse. This will include the impact of intergenerational trauma. Students will explore theories related to addiction and mental health and develop competencies in collaborative case management and intervention across the range of environments in which social workers engage with these areas of practice.

subject: SLWK
title: Indigenous Knowledges and Practices in Social Work
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course provides social work students with an introductory understanding of Indigenous ways of knowing and helping. Topics covered include, traditional Indigenous life-ways, worldviews, the role of spirit, significance of relationships, connection to the past, emphasis on community, respect for cultural practices, and the role of Elders within Indigenous helping practices. Students will gain an understanding of the history of social work interventions with Indigenous peoples, including residential schools, the sixties scoop, and strategies for effective engagement and collaboration with Indigenous children, families, and communities.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: SLWK
title: Social Work Practicum I
description: Lecture Hour(s): 1.5 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Seminar Other Hour(s): 28 Other Hours Schedule Type: Practicum Social Work Practicum I provides students with an opportunity to experience direct practice with individuals, groups, or communities. Practicum is intended to expose students to the complexity of practice issues related to work in human services and to view social issues from a social work perspective. During this first practicum experience students are expected to integrate learning from their first year courses with a focus on the application of social work skills, values and ethics. Students are challenged to examine how their social location influences their assessments of client situations and their work with clients or client groups. Students are expected to demonstrate a beginning understanding of social work as a distinct profession. Practicum seminars will be delivered using a blended delivery format, including both in-person and online meetings. Accreditation standards require that students are supervised by a Registered Social Worker resulting in tutorials that may be scheduled outside of regular class time. Students are required to complete 350 combined hours of fieldwork and seminar in order to successfully complete this course. In practicums where supervision is not provided by a registered social worker, students will be required to attend additional seminars to receive clinical supervision.
notes: SLWK 1187 can be taken either as a prerequisite or as a corequisite.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: SLWK
title: Social Work Practicum II
description: Lecture Hour(s): 1.5 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Seminar Other Hour(s): 28 Other Hours Schedule Type: Practicum Social Work Practicum II builds on the skills and knowledge from Social Work Practicum I with a goal of exposure to and involvement in more complex practice situations. Students integrate social work theories and methods in work with individuals, families, groups, and/or communities. Students are expected to demonstrate social work practice skills related to assessment, shared participation in the client change process, and interventions with a variety of client groups. Reflective practice and a focus on professionalism, social work ethics, and the standards of practice contribute to the continued development of social work identity. Practicum seminars will be delivered using a blended delivery format, including both in-person and online meetings. The Social Work diploma is governed by accreditation standards set by the Alberta College of Social Workers. Students are required to complete 350 combined hours of fieldwork and seminar in order to successfully complete this course. In practicums where supervision is not provided by a registered social worker, students will be required to attend additional seminars to receive clinical supervision
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: SLWK
title: Child and Adolescent Mental Health
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course provides students with an understanding of the many issues that affect the mental health of children and adolescents, including knowledge of the major mental health disorders, factors contributing to these disorders, and therapeutic interventions. The course also looks at the environments that optimize mental health and resilience. Consideration is given to the ecological factors that impact resilience and risk, how safety is enhanced, and the ways in which formal and natural supports are used to support positive outcomes in mental health and substance use. The course makes use of a simulation lab and naturalistic observation of children to explore how child and adolescent mental health issues are managed within a family or caregiving environment.

subject: SLWK
title: Diversity, Equity and Inclusion as Anti-oppressive Practice in Social Work
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course explores the history of diversity in Canadian social systems. The curriculum will assist students in understanding the causes of discrimination, oppression, poverty, exclusion, exploitation, and marginalization and their impacts on individuals, families, groups, and communities. Students will examine a broad range of critical theories including structural, feminist, anti-racist, queer, and anti-oppressive practice. Students will have the opportunity to develop their capacity for self-reflection and a critical understanding of power, privilege, and social location as it relates to professional social work practice.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: SLWK
title: Human Development and Social Contexts
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture Recognizing development as a process embedded within a social context, students will gain an understanding of how a range of life events and processes influence human development. Utilizing a prevention lens, students will explore protective factors that promote resilience during adverse developmental contexts. Attention will be given to how family, community and cultural systems enhance human development by looking at the ecological influences and the ways in which healing and strengths influence development.

subject: SLWK
title: Social Work Theories and Practice to Support Change
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This advanced social work course explores theories of social work practice with particular attention to those that promote change. Emphasis is on the application of theories through the development of interventions for direct social work practice. Students will be prepared to critically appraise and apply social work knowledge, as well as knowledge from other disciplines, to advance professional practice, policy development, and service provision.

subject: SLWK
title: Social Work Practice II
description: Lecture Hour(s): 1.5 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Seminar Other Hour(s): 25 Other Hours Schedule Type: Field Work Social Work Practice II builds on the skills and knowledge from Social Work Practice I with a goal of exposure to and involvement in more complex practice situations. Students integrate social work theories and methods in work with individuals, children and families, groups, or communities. Students are expected to demonstrate social work practice skills related to assessment, shared participation in the client change process, and interventions with a variety of client groups. A focus on reflective practice, professionalism, and social work ethics contribute to the continued development of social work identity. Students are required to complete 350 combined hours of fieldwork and seminars to successfully complete this course. In practicums where supervision is not provided by a registered social worker, students will be required to attend additional seminars to receive clinical supervision.
area_guess: Area I — Domains of Meaning & Value

subject: SLWK
title: Social Work Theories and Practice to Support Change in Child Intervention
description: Lecture Hour(s): 3 Lecture Hours Schedule Type: Lecture This course serves as an introduction to theories of child intervention practice. Particular emphasis is given to understanding the role of child protection in society and its place in protecting children while enhancing family functioning.
